Title: Comment from Goyette, Clare
Comment:
I have been a Care Credit customer for close to 2 decades.  It was first introduced to me by my dentist.  The use of Care Credit allowed me to have much needed dental work and pay for it over time.  

Cared Credit helped me cover almost $5000 worth of surgeries and treatments for my Boxer.  I have successfully used the product by paying as agreed and in full at the conclusion of the term. 
Recently, I had to use Care Credit for $1250 at my Vet. My little sick kitty had to have a number of tests, blood work and ultrasound.  At the end, he was very sick and I had to put him to sleep.  I could use Care Credit or any other credit card, but chose Care Credit for the option of a fixed payment. 

Why is Care Credit any different than Rooms to Go, Home Depot, Lowes, Best Buy or any other business that offers a Zero Interest program for a fixed monthly payment and fixed period.  

Read the fine print and use the product(s) appropriately.

Title: Comment from Brosseau, Spencer
Comment: A common way to pay for qualified medical expenses is through an employers Flexible Spending Account (FSA). This allows employees to contribute tax free to an account, usually administered by a third-party, and then use those funds to pay for qualified medical expenses. A significant issue that is common amongst Flexible Spending Accounts is that many have a use-it-or-lose-it provision and funds contributed that are not used by the end of a certain time period, typically the end of a plan year, are forfeited. This is a disincentive for people to use Flexible Spending Accounts for health related problems because most people are not able to forecast medical issues unless they are recurring. It would be beneficial to review this policy in the case of medical issues to help more people reduce the burden of unexpected medical costs. Many Health Savings Accounts (HSA) already allow yearly carry over so it would make sense to have similar expectations for Flexible Spending Accounts.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment: I have been using CareCredit for a little over a year, since late June, 2022 and honestly wish I had never gotten involved. I felt pressured into signing up for CareCredit by an emergency vet clinic, before diagnostics were completed and I was informed my pet had extremely limited chances of recovery. I ended up with a debt of a little under $5,000. There was an “interest free” period of only 6 months, but it ended up not mattering at all, as in 7 months, I have been charged over $1,300 in interest. I felt as if I’ve been taken advantage of by Synchronity bank, the entity that issues this card. I would not have signed up for such a card if I hadn’t been in the emotional state I was in, and very much feel taken advantage of by the issuing bank.

Title: Comment from Ashkenaz, Peter
Comment:
I am pleased to see the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and other federal agencies requesting information regarding medical credit cards. I hope that any proposed and final regulations prioritize the needs and actions of actual people.

Please see attached file for full comments
Attachment Files_extracted:
Peter Ashkenaz
Docket No. CFPB-2023-0038

I am pleased to see the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and other federal agencies requesting information regarding medical credit cards. I hope that any proposed and final regulations prioritize the needs and actions of actual people.

Taken from our own experiences, the role for medical credit cards occurs when someone is truly in need (sort of like making decisions about a funeral without ability to plan ahead). People who require a medical credit card are those who:
Are usually trying to figure out how to pay for medical or dental needs “right now”;
Have no support to make a good or informed decision (most Americans are not financially proficient and are even worse under stress); 
Want to cover their bills and don’t intend or want to default on payments;
Usually are living paycheck to paycheck where even a new bill will hurt financially and cause emotional stress;
Are generally extended on other credit cards and tend to make only the minimum payment, not thinking about the deferred interest requirements of any card and how that affects their credit or their total amount due;
Looking for ways to pay off their credit card bills, but are caught in the credit spiral; and
Want to make sure the provider they are seeing gets paid for rendering service so they will continue to be seen by their provider.

None of these concerns can truly be addressed through public policy. Still, the request for information and any subsequent rulemaking that attempts to address policy solutions will most likely result in locking in interest rates or possibly putting more hurdles directly on those individuals the regulations purport to help. Any of these regulatory efforts will only make it harder to customers and patients to do what they and their providers need and want: to pay their health-related bills. 

My Personal Experience
When we applied for a medical credit card, we met most of the items listed above. In the 1980’s we filed for bankruptcy protection as a result of high medical bills we were unable to cover. While we did come out from bankruptcy, continuing health care costs made it even harder to make ends meet. We were enrolled in fee-for-service health plans which meant we had to pay the providers upfront and wait as long as 90 days for reimbursement. Guess what that did to our cash flow with one of us living with a number of chronic conditions? 

Even before applying for the Care Credit card we had used one of our Visa cards to cover physical therapy costs; by the time the insurance payments of only 40% came in, we were unable to cover all the PT costs, thereby building up a hefty balance. 

Then a new dental emergency came about and we just didn’t have the savings to cover the new costs. The dental practice would not provide the care until the payment would be made so we applied for and were approved by Care Credit. We knew that if we didn’t pay it off within the 12-month deferral period the interest rate would skyrocket, but when that time came, we were still only able to cover the minimum costs. So now we had two credit cards paying for health care expenses, along with other expenses. 

I believe the weaknesses in the system is the overall cost of health care. Although the health insurance market has improved over the years, more and more providers are accepting insurance at the point of care resulting in fewer people have to pay all the costs out of pocket. But for many who tried to save money by buying bare-bones plans or health savings accounts, the cost of catastrophic care is when the cash register begins ringing. 

Medical Credit Cards in Real Life
As reported by the CFPB, medical credit cards are not like bank credit cards because of their use requirements. One doesn’t really have the ability to compare the differences in medical credit cards at the point of sale, like when a customer is being pushed into signing up for an affinity card at a retail chain (“if you sign up for the [store name] card, you will immediately get a 20% discount off your purchase today and you can defer interest for [# of months].”). Yet for a patient, they may not be thinking of what happens in 12 months: they need the care today.

Many of the public comments being published in news stories look at how medical credit cards are helping to pay hospital costs, but the problem is much wider – it is all health and health-related costs: the cost of glasses, dental care, physical therapy services; not just a doctor visit or hospital visit, or costs of prescriptions and anything needed as a medical device or other therapeutic. A perfect candidate for a medical credit card would be someone with physical limitations who not only needs to pay for medical visits, physical therapy, and prescriptions, but also needs to see an occupational therapist and purchase devices to help manage life needs, such as cooking, dressing, and bathing. All of these costs can be an unwelcome shock that no one ever truly prepares for, or for their family members. 

It has been established that outside of Medicare, most health insurers do not pay health care providers timely. A medical credit card resolves that problem. Our experience found that dentists and many independent physical therapists don’t accept any insurance, or the insurance payments are so low they require additional payments up front from patients, so the medical credit cards ensure they will be paid for providing care. Since health care is challenged by both access and affordability, solving at least one part of the challenge can help many people get the care they need. Having a medical credit card can alleviate at least one of these challenges for many people, as long as using the card doesn’t result in additional unintended consequences, such as a decline in one’s credit rating or additional stress-related conditions. 

Regulatory Solutions?
First in any regulatory solution is managing health care costs, especially at the point of care for patients. Health care consumers are not usually well-informed about their health care or the actual costs they may incur. While Medicare and other insurers have been looking at ways to make costs more transparent, that transparency will not reflect the actual cost a patient may be required to pay for a specific treatment. Furthermore, if a provider tells a patient that a certain treatment is required, there are very few times when someone will actually be able to shop around for a better price because that means finding a new provider, being examined by that provider and then getting the price from them. In the current health care market, just getting a new appointment can takes weeks, if not months, even when someone lives in an area where multiple providers accept new patients, and by then a health issue will most likely be exacerbated. A patient is not going to go online in an ambulance and ask the EMTs to take them to the hospital with the lowest costs, nor even worry about how to pay for the ambulance trip.  

Second, there are no strong policy authorities in place – the current legislative proposal, the Credit Card Competition Act of 2023, will not address any of the concerns raised by this request for information. The proposed legislation is designed to implement competition in the credit card networks in an effort to reduce costs for merchants which could possibly lower costs for consumers (but most likely won’t). The proposed legislation does not appear to impact medical credit cards.

I suggest that any regulatory actions require additional training of health provider staff before allowing them to offer patients an application for a medical credit card. Staff should be trained to help patients investigate or even apply for other financial aid programs before suggesting the medical credit card. I would be surprised if a practice would ever trains staff to be financial planners, but in an effort to care for patients, financial needs must be addressed along with their health care needs. 

HHS can propose requirements for health care providers to better explain costs under specific diagnostic codes to the patient and the impacts of waiting for care, similar to how some clinicians actually look at a patient’s insurance plan’s drug formulary to see if a medicine is covered under the plan. This would fit under existing rules requiring providers to post information about consumers’ rights, and would also advance the current focus on patient-centered care and shared decision-making. It would be impossible for patients or providers to have honest conversations about health choices when the prices for services are hidden and patients have no way to make informed decisions. Significant changes in banking and financial services occurred over the past decades to protect consumers but no consideration has been taken to look at the impact of health care costs on patients at the point-of-care. 

While this request for information is a good first step, it is only part of the wide range of policy improvements that consumers need to make health care and financial decisions today, especially since health care and financial decisions are closely intertwined.

Title: Comment from Berger, Mitchelll
Comment:
Dear Mr. Carare: In response to the above RFI, I write to encourage CFPB, Treasury and HHS to further study the implications of use of such medical payment products as specialty credit cards and installment financing among patients receiving treatment for mental health conditions and substance use disorders. Patients being treatment for behavioral health conditions may be more likely than others to have medical debt and may have trouble finding treatment fully covered by insurance, leading to use of other available payment approaches such as credit cards. Thank you for consideration of these views. 
Sincerely, Mitchell Berger

To: Octavian Carare, Supervisory Economist, Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, at 
Octavian.Carare@cfpb.gov or (202) 435–7700 
 
From: Mitchell Berger, (comments made in personal capacity), mazruia@hotmail.com 
 
Re: Request for Information (RFI) Regarding Medical Payment Products, July 12, 2023,

medical-payment-products 
 
Dear Mr. Carare: In response to the above RFI, I write to encourage CFPB, Treasury and HHS to further 
study the implications of use of such medical payment products as specialty credit cards and installment 
financing among patients receiving treatment for mental health conditions and substance use disorders. 
Patients being treatment for behavioral health conditions may be more likely than others to have medical 
debt and may have trouble finding treatment fully covered by insurance, leading to use of other available 
payment approaches such as credit cards.1 
 
According to the 2021 National Survey on Drug Use and Health (the most recent survey available),2 46.3 
million Americans aged 12 and older had a substance use disorder and 57.8 million adults age 18 and 
older had a mental illness. Of these adults, roughly 14.1 million American adults had a serious mental 
illness which is differentiated from any mental illness based on causing serious functional impairment. 
Roughly 25 percent of those aged 12 and older with an untreated substance use disorder cite the cost of 
care as a key barrier to accessing treatment. Similarly, roughly 48 percent of adults with any mental 
illness and 54 percent of those with a serious mental illness cited cost of care as the key obstacle 
preventing their access to treatment.  
 
One factor contributing to these cost of care barriers is that many behavioral health professionals do not 
accept insurance, which in turn drives higher out-of-pocket costs for patients.3 Installment plans, medical 
credit cards and other approaches may be needed to defray these costs. Some behavioral health companies 
online expressly recommend use of products mentioned in the CFBP/HHS/Treasury RFI for addiction 
treatment or other behavioral health services. 
 
One 2020 Psychiatric Services study found that fewer than 6 percent of active practicing psychiatrists in 
Massachusetts filed insurance claims for more than 300 patients per year. The authors comment that 
“[e]ven in areas with relatively large numbers of psychiatrists, such as Massachusetts, most psychiatrists 
accept private health insurance for only a few patients per year, and few psychiatrists meet even 
conservative definitions of a full outpatient caseload of patients billed through private health insurance. 
We found that many psychiatrists who participate in the insurance market see very few patients with 
insurance reimbursement, which suggests that counting psychiatrists alone could grossly overestimate the 
supply available to patients who cannot afford to pay out of pocket.”4 Psychiatrists may receive lower 
reimbursement than other physicians even when providing similar treatments.5 The situation for other 
behavioral health providers is similar. Various studies note that: only about one-third of psychologists

accepting private insurance;6 government and private insurance often place limitations on addiction 
treatment;7 and in some states more than 40 percent of marriage and family therapists do not accept 
insurance.8 
 
These factors may contribute to use of medical payment products for mental health or substance use 
disorder treatment particularly for residential treatment or for what some view as innovative treatments 
that have yet to be FDA-approved.9 There also have been allegations that some substance use centers have 
encouraged use of loans or other financing not necessarily in the patient’s long-term best interests.10  
 
Overall, I think better understanding is needed of how these products are used in behavioral health 
settings and potential benefits and drawbacks for patients, families/caregivers and behavioral health 
providers. It would be helpful as well to better understand use of such products for residential behavioral 
health treatment; disparities in use of medical payment products for behavioral health care based on 
geography (rural, suburban, urban), race/ethnicity and income levels; and long-term impacts of use of 
credit cards, installment plans and other payment products for patients. Also important is better 
understanding the use of potential alternatives to pay for some forms of behavioral health care such as 
health savings accounts, which tend to be underused by many consumers.11 Raising awareness among 
providers of tools to promote patient financial security, such as lower income patient eligibility for federal 
and state tax credits,  can help perhaps in some cases to reduce patient need for less flexible financial 
tools to pay for medical expenses for behavioral health or other services.12 I appreciate your consideration 
of these views.

RE: Docket No. CFPB–2023–0038 
 
Dear Director Chopra, 
 
I respectfully submit this letter on behalf of the American Association of Dental Office 
Management (AADOM). AADOM is the nation’s largest professional association for dental office 
managers and practice administrators. We have worked with CareCredit for many years. 
CareCredit’s access to care aligns with AADOM’s values.

members. Over my 20 years as president of AADOM, I have worked with innumerable dental 
office managers and practice administrators who have responsibly utilized CareCredit in their 
practices and they are grateful it has been an option for patients to access care they otherwise could

Patients have out-of-pocket expenses for medical care, including dentistry, and not all patients 
have sufficient insurance or cash on hand to pay for the dental work they want or need at the time 
of service.  
 
So many dentists are true caregivers at their core, business owners or business associates 
secondarily. Dental practices must remain profitable to remain in business to continue to provide 
necessary healthcare. To that end, offering payment options such as CareCredit is both responsible 
and necessary.  
 
I urge your committee to reconsider your stance on practical, responsible credit options for patients 
and their medical/dental needs.  
 
Sincerely,

Title: Comment from Powell, Rebecca
Comment: This is another example of why we need universal healthcare in the United States. Our current system allows prices to skyrocket (we spend more than any other developed country on healthcare costs, and for really relatively little benefit), and most bankruptcies are related to medical debt. This new practice of just signing patients up on admittance into finance contracts will put more people in financial jeopardy, and hospitals signing contracts with for-profit finance companies makes me very uncomfortable. I disapprove of this interest-bearing financing of medical bills.

Title: Comment from Unell, Alan
Comment:
If there were universal healthcare in the US we would not be having a discussion on financing health care debt at the point of service.  Most bankruptcies in the US are related to medical debt.  This predatory activity of pushing patients into finance contracts will only make matters worse for patients and their families and increase the number of bankruptcies.  Hospitals used to set up payment plans when needed, often at no interest, because they had the appearance of operating for the public good.

I strongly disapprove of this interest bearing financing of medical bills

Title: Comment from Land, Mark
Comment: My dentist has offered me a medical credit card to help finance a $5,000 medical dental repair that is not covered by my insurance. I declined their offer,  My dentist  acted offended that I would not use the credit card financing.

Title: Comment from Koerner, Kenny
Comment: There are many different types of medical payment products. I'd be careful to lump them all into the same category.  My organization partners with a local bank to provide financing to our patients for up to 72 months at an interest rate of between 4%-6%. Offering the financing to our patients is not our first option by any means, it is just there as another option for our patients in addition to applying for financial assistance, making no interest payments that based upon patient balance run for up to 24 months in length, and then as a final option to offer, the financing for up to 72 months.  Since Medicare and Medicaid and now Medicare Advantage don't even pay hospitals and organizations the cost of providing services, it is imperative that hospitals are able to collect patient due amount portion of the healthcare services provided. I am asking that all forms of medical payment products are highlighted such as the product we are using at our organization.

Title: Comment submitted by Kelly Griffith
Comment: See Attached
Attachment Files_extracted:
509 E Radburn Street, Tucson, AZ 85704 
www.economicintegrity.org 
Phone (520) 250-4416 
 
 
August 21, 2023 
To: 
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 
1700 G Street, NW 
Washington, DC 20552 
 
Re: 
Docket No. CFPB–2023–0038  
Response to CFPB request for information on medical debt 
 
In November 2022, Arizona voters, by an overwhelming 72 percent to 28 percent, 
approved a new law that restricts the interest on medical debt to no more than three 
percent1,2.  This was the first statewide ballot measure in the nation to restrict interest rates on 
medical debt, and it was widely seen as a model for other states. However, almost immediately, 
a huge loophole has come into focus. 
The Center for Economic Integrity became aware of the loophole after a volunteer 
noticed a card on the desk of a receptionist at Banner Health3 promoting a health-care line of 
credit of up to $10,000 offered by a company named Curae4. The volunteer wondered how 
Curae could make a profit on a three-percent loan. However, his visit to Curae’s website 
revealed the company wasn’t charging three percent, but annual interest rates of 9.9 percent 
to 36 percent5. 
Additionally, should a consumer make a late payment, it triggers an interest rate of 
29.99 percent that is accrued from the first day the money was borrowed. This penalty rate is 
charged until at least six consecutive minimum payments are made when due. This penalty 
applies even to no-interest loans if they are not paid off in time. 
                                                      
1 “Arizona Proposition 209, Healthcare Debt Interest Rate Limit and Debt Collection Exemptions Initiative (2022)” 
https://ballotpedia.org/Arizona_Proposition_209,_Healthcare_Debt_Interest_Rate_Limit_and_Debt_Collection_Exemptions_Initiative_(2022)  
2 “Proposition 209 Information Sheet”, at AZCourts.gov,  
https://www.azcourts.gov/selfservicecenter/Garnishment/Proposition-209  
3 Banner Health is an $8.7 trillion revenue health-care nonprofit headquartered in Phoenix, Arizona. 
https://www.bannerhealth.com/  
4 Curae is a subsidiary of Atlanticus Holdings (ATLC), a public company traded on Nasdaq and headquartered in 
Atlanta, Georgia.   
5 “Pricing Information Addendum” and “Cardholder Agreement”, accessed 08/18/2023. 
http://curae.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/Curae-Cardholder-Agreement-and-Pricing-Addendum.pdf  

 
2 
The loophole is that once the consumer takes out the loan to pay his or her medical bill, 
the debt is no longer medical debt. It is now credit debt, and the three percent interest rate no 
longer applies.  
HealthCare Rising Arizona, the primary sponsor of the voter initiative, won the first 
round of litigation over the new law6.  Now the case is in the appeals process. However, in the 
meantime state officials have made no effort to apply the three percent restriction to such 
loans taken out to pay medical expenses.  
The Center for Economic Integrity believes that such loans violate the spirit of the new 
Arizona law, which was passed to ensure no Arizona family should lose their home or car due to 
medical emergencies or accidents or be trapped into unending debt by unfair interest rates on 
medical care.  
But the provisions of Curae’s loan does exactly that – it traps borrowers. Its low or zero-
percent initial interest rate appeals to patients, but its penalty rate unfairly traps borrowers 
unfortunate enough to make a late payment into high interest rate debt that dramatically 
increases the cost of the patient’s health care. 
Arizona’s experience should be a cautionary tale to any other state that seeks to limit 
the interest rate on medical debt. It needs to make sure that debt incurred for medical care, 
even with credit cards or loans, is covered by its law restricting the interest on medical debt. 
Otherwise, their new law will be virtually meaningless. 
 
 
 
Kelly S, Griffith 
Executive Director,  
Center for Economic Integrity 
                                                      
6 https://www.azcentral.com/story/news/politics/arizona/2022/12/22/arizona-proposition-209-is-not-
unconstitutionally-vague-judge-rules/69735224007/

BILLING RIGHTS NOTICE 
 
 
Your Billing Rights: Keep This Document For Future Use 
This notice tells you about your rights and our responsibilities under the Fair Credit Billing Act. 
What To Do If You Find A Mistake On Your Statement 
If you think there is an error on your statement, write to us at: 
Account Services Dispute Resolution  
P.O. Box 105555 
Atlanta, GA  30348-5555  
In your letter, give us the following information: 
• 
Account information:  Your name and account number. 
• 
Dollar amount:  The dollar amount of the suspected error. 
• 
Description of problem:  If you think there is an error on your bill, describe what you believe is wrong and why you 
believe it is a mistake. 
You must contact us: 
• 
Within 60 days after the error appeared on your statement. 
• 
At least 3 business days before an automated payment is scheduled, if you want to stop payment on the amount you 
think is wrong. 

 
 
You must notify us of any potential errors in writing.  You may call us, but if you do we are not required to investigate any 
potential errors and you may have to pay the amount in question. 
What Will Happen After We Receive Your Letter 
When we receive your letter, we must do two things: 
1.  Within 30 days of receiving your letter, we must tell you that we received your letter.  We will also tell you if we have 
already corrected the error. 
2.  Within 90 days of receiving your letter, we must either correct the error or explain to you why we believe the bill is 
correct. 
While we investigate whether or not there has been an error: 
• 
We cannot try to collect the amount in question, or report you as delinquent on that amount. 
• 
The charge in question may remain on your statement, and we may continue to charge you interest on that amount. 
• 
While you do not have to pay the amount in question, you are responsible for the remainder of your balance. 
• 
We can apply any unpaid amount against your credit limit.  
After we finish our investigation, one of two things will happen: 
• 
If we made a mistake:  You will not have to pay the amount in question or any interest or other fees related to that 
amount. 
• 
If we do not believe there was a mistake:  You will have to pay the amount in question, along with applicable interest 
and fees.  We will send you a statement of the amount you owe and the date payment is due.  We may then report you as 
delinquent if you do not pay the amount we think you owe. 
If you receive our explanation but still believe your bill is wrong, you must write to us within 10 days telling us that you still 
refuse to pay.  If you do so, we cannot report you as delinquent without also reporting that you are questioning your bill.  We 
must tell you the name of anyone to whom we reported you as delinquent, and we must let those organizations know when the 
matter has been settled between us. 
If we do not follow all of the rules above, you do not have to pay the first $50 of the amount you question even if your bill is 
correct. 
Your Rights If You Are Dissatisfied With Your Credit Card Purchases 
If you are dissatisfied with the goods or services that you have purchased with your credit card, and you have tried in good 
faith to correct the problem with the merchant, you may have the right not to pay the remaining amount due on the purchase. 
To use this right, all of the following must be true: 
1.  The purchase must have been made in your home state or within 100 miles of your current mailing address, and the 
purchase price must have been more than $50.  (Note: Neither of these are necessary if your purchase was based on an 
advertisement we mailed to you, or if we own the company that sold you the goods or services.) 
2.  You must have used your credit card for the purchase. Purchases made with cash advances from an ATM or with a 
check that accesses your credit card account do not qualify. 
3.  You must not yet have fully paid for the purchase. 
If all of the criteria above are met and you are still dissatisfied with the purchase, contact us in writing at: 
Account Services Dispute Resolution  
P.O. Box 105555 
Atlanta, GA  30348-5555  
While we investigate, the same rules apply to the disputed amount as discussed above. After we finish our investigation, we 
will tell you our decision.  At that point, if we think you owe an amount and you do not pay, we may report you as delinquent.

Title: Comment from Noreen, Kristin
Comment:
In 2010 (13 years ago today, in fact), I was hit by a car on my bike at age 46. I had 15 fractures, a traumatic brain injury, internal organ damage and an amputated hand (with successful reattachment). I was treated at PeaceHealth St. Joseph Hospital in Bellingham, Washington, for 8 weeks, during which my bills were over $1 million. Those were covered by insurances and a charitable grant, all but $9,000, which came out of my pocket. The kicker, though, was aftercare. I will need chiropractic, massage and acupuncture care for the rest of my life. These things are minimally, if at all, covered by insurance. The only way to pay for these uncovered services is by credit card if I don't have the money up front, and I usually don't, because I can only work part time and I don't qualify for disability. My income is $25,000 a year, I pay $7,000 in self-employment tax, and my credit cards currently have a $12,000 balance, which is all medical debt. I pay about $200 a month in interest on this balance, and there's no end in sight. No ship coming in. No mercy if I can't make my payments. After all, Capital One doesn't care if I spent the money getting blowouts at Gene Juarez or getting a dislocated rib popped back in place. 
I filed bankruptcy in 2016 to discharge $56,000 in medical debt. I lost my condominium too, and I now live in a tiny house on wheels in someone's backyard. 
There needs to be protection for people who are backed into this corner, or better yet, we shouldn't have to pay out of our own pockets for these services. Medical debt is crushing people.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment:
I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the services provided by Care Credit. My initial encounter with the company was during a dentist appointment when I was experiencing tooth pain. They bated me and painted the card like it was perfect for me.
However, my experience since then has been nothing short of disappointing. Not only has my balance of $700 not significantly decreased despite making consistent and timely payments, but it has actually risen to $638 over the course of 10+ months. Even though I sometimes pay more than the minimum due, the balance inexplicably continues to climb.
What's more concerning is the feeling that I can no longer trust a company that pushes their credit card on individuals, seemingly prioritizing profit over customer satisfaction. This loss of trust was solidified when I opted to forgo a recommended dental treatment during a subsequent appointment simply because I couldn't trust a company that operates in such a manner.
I am sharing this experience in the hope that others will be cautious when considering Care Credit's services. My intention is not only to vent my frustration but also to shed light on the less-than-ideal practices that seem to be the norm for this company. Transparent and fair financial solutions should be at the heart of any reputable business, and unfortunately, I found that lacking in my dealings with Care Credit.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment: So far I've been fortunate enough not to fall into medical debt. However, the cost of health insurance is so ridiculously high that in the past I've gone for several years without insurance and just prayed that I wouldn't get into a car accident or be diagnosed with a disease like cancer. Even with insurance, I don't always go to my doctor when it would probably be a good idea because my deductible is a few thousand dollars. Now that my husband and I are newly retired and likely to need more medical care in the coming years, we're seriously considering moving to another country that has excellent medical care for a reasonable cost. It would be an absolute nightmare to be elderly and bankrupted by our ludicrous medical system, particularly since my husband and I have no children who would be able to help care for us if we ran out of money.

Title: Comment from Verret, Jude
Comment:
I am writing to express my sincere concern and request regarding the availability of deferred payment options on credit cards. As a responsible credit card user, I believe that these options play a crucial role in providing financial flexibility and security to consumers like me.

Deferred payment plans provide a significant level of flexibility and financial relief to individuals facing unexpected expenses, emergencies, or temporary financial hardships. Such options help consumers manage their finances more effectively, preventing them from falling into undue stress or potential debt traps. It is my belief that allowing these options to remain accessible will greatly contribute to the overall well-being of consumers.

I routinely utilize deferred interest options for medical procedures that health insurance does not cover or necessary veterinary services. If it would not be for deferred interest options, I would be forced to utilize standard credit card terms that include exorbitant interest rates.

I understand the importance of responsible lending practices and the need to ensure consumers are well-informed about the terms and conditions of deferred payment plans. However, I would like to emphasize that with appropriate transparency and clear communication, these options can continue to benefit both consumers and financial institutions alike.

In conclusion, I kindly urge the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to consider the positive impact that deferred payment options have on consumers' financial stability. I believe that maintaining these options aligns with the principles of consumer protection and empowerment.

Thank you for your time and consideration of this matter.

Jude Verret

Title: Comment from Colorado Children's Campaign
Comment:
My name is Hunter Nelson, and I am a Senior Policy Analyst at the Colorado Children’s Campaign. Together with our partner organizations and communities across the state, we advocate for the development and implementation of data-driven public policy that improves child and family well-being. We fight for a world in which, without exception, public policies and investments remove barriers for most impacted children and families and improve well-being for every child and every family. The Children’s Campaign recognizes the devastating impacts of medical debt on families and is in strong support of eliminating predatory medical loan practices that negatively impact families’ economic stability. 

Medical debt is a major problem in Colorado. According to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (2022), approximately 1 in 8 Coloradans hold medical debt in collections, amounting to a total sum of more than $1.3 million. Medical debt also disproportionately impacts households with children – according to the Census Bureau, households with children under the age of 18 (24.7%) were more likely to have medical debt than households without children (16.5%).  Birthing people are also more likely to have medical debt. Women are more likely to report having medical debt than men, and 1 in 8 American households report that pregnancy and/or birth costs contributed to their medical debt.  

Medical debt can have crippling impacts on families with children, causing families to forgo essential needs such as food, clothing and rental payments, spending down their savings to pay for medical bills, borrowing money from family members or friends, and taking on additional debts.  Also, when medical debt is included in consumer reports, which are frequently pulled by employers and landlords and can significantly impact the decision on whether to offer employment or housing to an applicant. The inclusion of medical debt on these reports can negatively impact a family‘s credit score, which can have rippling effects on subsequent employment and housing opportunities. 

According to a 2020 AIMS Public Health report, people with medical debt are three times more likely to have mental health symptoms such as depression, anxiety and stress. Not having to worry about medical debt and its inclusion on consumer reports has the potential to promote better mental and emotional health outcomes among families nationwide.

The Children’s Campaign believes that every family with children in Colorado and nationwide should have the resources and opportunities to achieve economic prosperity, and eliminating predatory medical loan practices that cause families to accrue enormous amounts of medical debt is one step toward making this a reality for all.  

Thank you,

Hunter Nelson
Senior Policy Analyst 
Hunter@coloradokids.org 

i. Medical Debt Burden in the United States (consumerfinance.gov) 
ii. Who Had Medical Debt in the United States? (census.gov)
iii. The burden of medical debt in the United States - Peterson-KFF Health System Tracker
iv. Ibid. 
v. Problems paying medical bills and mental health symptoms post-Affordable Care Act - PMC (nih.gov)

Date 8-25-2023 
      Subject Line: Docket No. CFPB–2023–0038 
      Salutation: Director Chopra, 
The Hispanic Dental Association (“HDA”) is pleased to submit these comments in response to the 
Request for Information issued by the Department of Health and Human Services, the Department of 
Treasury, and the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (the “Agencies”) (“RFI”). HDA is the leading voice 
for Hispanic Oral Health, providing service, education-research, advocacy, and leadership for all healthcare 
professionals to promote the overall health of the Hispanic/Latinx and underrepresented communities.  
We are driven by our vision to empower healthcare professionals to increase oral health equity and 
improve health care quality in the Hispanic/Latinx and underrepresented populations.1 
It is the HDA’s mission to promote the overall health of the Hispanic/Latinx and other 
underrepresented communities, and we offer these comments with that in mind.  Our members strongly 
support the goal of ensuring fair treatment to our patients, both by health care providers as well as by 
financial services providers.  We generally believe these objectives are being met, at least as it relates to 
the patients our members see on a daily basis.  With that in mind, we are concerned that the RFI suggests 
that providers, like dentists, habitually provide poor service to their patients as it relates to treatment, 
billing, insurance, and payments.  This is, of course, not the case as it relates to our members.  Dentistry is 
a word-of-mouth business, if you will pardon the pun, and it is not difficult for patients to leave a bad 
practice.  A dentist who treats his or her patients poorly is not likely to be a successful dentist.  To the 
extent the Agencies believe that there are improvements that can be made in the marketplace, the HDA 
stands ready to assist.  However, the apparent assumption that there are material deficiencies with how 
our members treat their patients, including how they may make financing options available, is not 
accurate. 
We also want to note that the RFI recognizes that dentists are part of a larger medical 
community providing essential medical services.  HDA strongly agrees with the Agencies on this point.  We 
believe that dentists’ status as medical providers providing critical care has been true since the outset of 
the profession.  This is an important fact because as to the government’s response to the COVID pandemic, 
one or more of the Agencies did not recognize the dental profession as providing essential or medical 
services. The government agencies in charge of healthcare missed the importance of oral healthcare 
providers in the management of the Covid Pandemic. But the deputy Surgeon General, who is the highest-
ranking oral Healthcare officer of our Government reach out to oral healthcare leaders and stakeholder 
along with key head of healthcare to coordinate efforts to collaborate and support the healthcare efforts 
which at the time needed our extra input and support. It was thanks to this initiative that Dentist started 
helping in the vaccination process that at first was not allowed due to misinformation by the US 
Healthcare branches. Because of this type of mishandling, although with good intentions, it concerns the 
Hispanic Dental Association that our communities would be negatively affected by your initiatives to 
interfere with one of the most innovative and powerful financial tools our members use to fight inequities 
in healthcare. Regardless, we are pleased to see that the Agencies appropriately deem dental care to be 
medical care. 
Turning more to the substance of the RFI, our experience indicates that consumers greatly 
appreciate the ability to use credit when paying for dental care.  This is true regardless of whether 
payment is made with a Visa or Mastercard, or with a product like CareCredit.  There are a variety of 
dental needs our patients have that may not be covered by insurance but are strongly desired and or 
needed.  Many patients could not afford the care if they had to pay by cash or check out-of-pocket.  For 
those consumers who are looking at their financial options, we are pleased that many of our members can 
suggest credit options like CareCredit.  There is no question that products like CareCredit make dental care 
more affordable and more available to more of our members’ patients.  We can say with certainty that 
many of our members’ patients would not be able to obtain appropriate dental care without access to 
CareCredit.  In fact, our members’ ability to offer access to various credit products aligns perfectly with our 
vision of increasing oral health equity and improving health care quality in the Hispanic/Latinx and other 
underrepresented communities.  Without access to credit, including CareCredit, access to dental care 
suffers. 
We urge the Agencies to consider the benefits our members’ patients receive when they can 
access credit.  HDA strongly believes that the cause of oral health equity is advanced through the use of 
CareCredit. 
Sincerely, 
 
Executive Director & Chief executive Officer 
Hispanic Dental Association

Director Chopra: 
On behalf of the American Med Spa Association (AmSpa), I am pleased to respond to the Request for Information 
Regarding Medical Payment Products (“RFI”) issued by the Department of Health and Human Services, the Department 
of Treasury, and the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (collectively, the “Agencies”).   
AmSpa is a professional trade association with 4,000 members nationwide. Our members consist of medical spas, their 
owners and employees, as well as much of the industry that serves them. AmSpa has been around for more than ten 
years and has a good sense of the issues that face this industry and the patient-base it serves.  
As the Agencies may know, medical spas provide elective, cash-based, non-invasive or minimally invasive aesthetic 
medical procedures designed to improve a patient’s physical appearance. These procedures and treatments can range 
from cosmetic injections such as hyaluronic acid dermal fillers, energy-based skin tightening, to body contouring, to laser 
or light hair removal.  Our members’ patients are looking for high quality care that improves their appearance and their 
confidence. 
AmSpa supports the Agencies’ goal to ensure that patients are treated well by their providers.  Perhaps more than most, 
AmSpa members recognize the need to provide a safe, compliant experience and exceed their patients’ expectations 
every day.  The care our members provide is voluntary and elective – medical spas accept cash for their services, and 
very few collect reimbursements from third-party payors. Since the care itself is discretionary, and patients have choices 
about who they see for care, a medical spa or medical aesthetic practice will not thrive if it does not provide appropriate 
care to its patients, including how it bills for care. And as you can imagine, medical spas need to accept payment 
securely, discretely, and electronically in a variety of different ways to meet the patients’ needs.  
Given the fact that AmSpa members do not provide the types of care described by the Agencies in the RFI (e.g., non-
elective care provided through hospitals), it does not appear the Agencies intend to delve into how patients pay for 
elective procedures, such as those offered through aesthetic practices.  I am not aware of deficiencies in the industry 
relating to billing or financing, and any attempt to regulate how a consumer pays a medical spa or aesthetic practice 
would be misguided. At a minimum, it seems appropriate for elective, cash-based procedures to be separated out from 
any rules regarding payment given that they fall into a distinct category that is very different from the issues addresses 
by the Agencies. 
Having said that, patients put a high value on the ability to use various forms of payment when paying for aesthetic care.  
AmSpa members are pleased to offer their patients the ability to use a variety of payment types because members know 
that each patient has different needs.  While some can pay for care up front with ready funds, many patients need to be 
able to spread the payments for care over time.  This could be through use of a credit card, for example.  While AmSpa 
members generally accept the universally known credit card brands like Visa or American Express, many also accept 
popular products like CareCredit. Our members accept credit cards, including CareCredit, because they know it gives 
their patients the flexibility to get the care they want.  Conversely, if the Agencies restricted patients’ access to credit, 
that would mean that patients would have to put off, delay, or forgo treatment altogether—especially for the services 
AmSpa members provide because they are elective and generally not covered by insurance.

Re: Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products 
 
To Whom it May Concern: 
 
On behalf of our 159,000 members, the American Dental Association (ADA) is writing in 
response to the joint Request for Information (RFI) by the Consumer Financial Protection 
Bureau (CFPB), Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), and Department of the 
Treasury (Treasury).  
 
The ADA has been a driving force in dentistry for more than 160 years and is committed to 
advancing the art and science of dentistry and the health of the public. Dentistry is essential 
healthcare, and oral health is connected to overall health. Neglecting dental care can lead to 
long-term systemic health issues. 
 
Dentistry, as a healthcare profession, is deeply committed to patient welfare and ethical 
practices. The relationship between dentists and their patients is built on trust, where patients’ 
needs and well-being take precedence. This commitment is grounded in the professional ethics 
and code of conduct developed by the American Dental Association upon which ADA member 
dentists agree to abide. 
 
For more than two decades, an ADA subsidiary has endorsed CareCredit to its members as a 
helpful financing option for patients regardless of their insurance status. Considering that dental 
plans often only cover 50% of the cost of major services such as dental implants, bridges and 
crowns meant to restore oral health and function, the out-of-pocket costs may deter patients 
from seeking this necessary treatment.  
 
Not all patients have immediate funds to cover either the full cost or out-of-pocket costs for their 
treatment, and credit options can bridge this gap to help ensure that necessary care is not 
hindered by financial constraints. Dental practices bear a cost to offer CareCredit to patients to 
ensure patients can access financing options tailored to their needs. This starts at a level that is 

August 31, 2023 
Page 2 
significantly higher than typical credit card interchange rates for six months of a no interest if 
paid in full plan and escalates from there for 12, 18, and 24 month no-interest plans with the 
cost to the dental practice, increasing the longer the period of no interest. The cost to dental 
practices to offer CareCredit’s no interest if paid in full financing terms to patients is significantly 
higher than the “merchant” fees paid on other credit cards, but many of our members provide 
this as an option to patients who find the promotional financing useful in getting the care they 
want with the ability to pay over time. 
 
Financing terms, including the consequences of late or missing payments and not paying within 
the specified time frame, are clearly explained in consumer-friendly language CareCredit 
provides to dental practices for patients when patients consider whether they wish to finance 
their care.  
 
Patients appreciate the availability of CareCredit to separate health care costs from other 
purchases they make on major credit cards which charge interest on monthly balances if not 
paid in full. Given that a sizable portion of Americans carry consumer card debt, CareCredit's 
no-interest if paid in full financing periods alleviates the burden of paying interest so long as the 
terms of the finance agreement are met. Approximately 115,000 dentists offer CareCredit to 
their patients, and in 2022 more than $5 billion was financed for dental treatment. Survey data 
indicates 98% of people that used CareCredit say it met or exceeded their expectations.  
 
Above all, dentists are concerned with their patients’ wellbeing. It is heartbreaking to see a 
patient’s reaction if they have a serious oral health condition and they wonder how they can pay 
for it even if they have dental insurance. When faced with the difficult decision of whether to 
forgo treatment or add the cost to existing credit card debt, CareCredit offers viable and 
reasonable patient financing.  
 
**** 
 
In summary, we appreciate the role of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau in this matter 
and want to assure the CFPB that trust between dentists and patients is of paramount concern. 
The ADA expresses its support of CareCredit as a practical financing option enabling patients to 
access dental care they may not otherwise be able to afford. 
 
Thank you again for the opportunity to offer input on these issues. Please do not hesitate to 
contact David Linn at linnd@ada.org with any questions.

Title: Comment from American Veterinary Medical Association
Comment: Please see the attached comment from the American Veterinary Medical Association. Thank you.
Attachment Files_extracted:
9/5/2023 
 
 
VIA WWW.REGULATIONS.GOV 
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 
1700 G Street NW 
Washington, DC 20552 
 
RE: 
Docket No. CFPB–2023–0038; Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products 
 
 
On behalf of the American Veterinary Medical Association, which represents more than 
100,000 member veterinarians in every US state and territory, I am very pleased to submit these 
comments, in response to Docket No. CFPB-2023-0038, Request for Information Regarding 
Medical Payment Products, related to credit financing options for owners of pets and other 
animals. 
Owning a pet is a wonderful aspect of the lives of many Americans. Indeed, 56.8% of 
U.S. households own a pet.1 It is important to note that when an animal requires veterinary 
care, the full cost of care is typically due at the time of service. This means that when an owner 
is confronted with a medical emergency or other situation requiring immediate surgery or other 
interventions for their animal, they are often faced with unexpected and unbudgeted 
expenditures. Many pet owners harbor concerns that they cannot pay an unexpected bill for 
veterinary medical care. Even an unplanned expenditure of approximately $1,000 would cause 
financial stress to a little over half of respondents in one major survey.2  
Pet health insurance can play a role in helping to defray the cost of veterinary medical 
care, and while the uptake of pet health insurance by animal owners is increasing, the minority 
of pets are insured. Insurance penetration varies by location and species, but industry estimates 
range from a high of 18.6% of pets in California, to 0.1% of pets in South Dakota3. Even for those 
who do have a pet health insurance policy, in some cases, the owner still must provide payment 
up front and then work with the insurance agency for reimbursement.  
Medical payment products, such as healthcare credit cards, help many animal owners 
receive veterinary medical care for their animals that they would not otherwise be able to 
obtain.  If medical payment products or other options are not available, then animal owners – 
depending upon the nature and severity of their animal’s problem – can be faced with the very 
difficult choice of foregoing needed treatment, or euthanizing a beloved family pet because 
they do not have the immediate financial means to provide the care (what is often referred to in 
veterinary medicine as “economic euthanasia”).  This is not an uncommon circumstance in 
veterinary medicine and can have a significant impact upon the animal owner. However, with a 
healthcare credit card financing option, the owner may be able to spread payments out over six 
months without interest (unlike non-medical credit cards), enabling treatment to proceed, 
 
1 AVMA 2017 Pet Ownership and Demographic Sourcebook 
2 Williams et.al, 2020 ‘The Impact of Pet Health Insurance on Dog Owners’ Spending for Veterinary Services’ Animals, 10.1162. 
3 NAPHIA 2023 State of the Industry Report 

2 
 
saving the pet’s life or preserving an animal’s quality of life.  Providing animal owners with the 
option to access medical payment products, which allow them additional time and financial 
flexibility that may be needed to manage the cost of care, serves the animal-owning public well. 
Health and wellness credit cards can therefore be a good option for some consumers in 
certain situations, enabling owners to be responsive to their pet’s or other animal’s needs 
without experiencing financial stress, and to work with their veterinarian to provide medically 
necessary veterinary care that may not otherwise be undertaken. This can also help veterinary 
healthcare teams, who are deeply impacted when animals under their care must be euthanized 
due to economic factors.   
We believe that providing a range of financing options for veterinary services is in the 
best interest of animals, their owners, and the veterinary teams that work to provide their 
patients and clients with optimal care and support. 
Thank you for the opportunity to share our comments, and please do not hesitate to 
contact us if you have any questions or would like more information. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely, 
 
Janet D. Donlin, DVM, CAE 
Chief Executive Officer 
American Veterinary Medical Association

Title: Comment from Fisher, Jacob
Comment:
I went to the ER of Seton Medical Center Harker Heights in May 2022when I was having heart palpitations. I figured it was from caffeine, but wanted to be safe with a family history of heart disease. I told the financial lady I would only pay the Medicare price for the visit. One month later I was billed $2,664 for an EKG and some labs. The Medicare price for an identical bill is $203. They billed it as a level 4 ER visit which is upcoding. They billed me for additional codes outside of the ER visit code which is code unbundling. I disputed the bill and sent a letter to the hospital, but they still sent me to debt collections. I disputed it to the first debt collections company and they dropped it. It was sent to a second debt collections company that reports to bureaus (damages credit scores). I disputed it and they dropped it months later. Currently it is with a third debt collections agency, which does not report to the credit agencies. Although, there is no threat to my credit score, I am sending a final dispute letter before I take the hospital to small claims court to settle this.

I work in health insurance and patient advocacy, and have disputed bills for others as well. In general, hospitals do not inform patients of their 501r financial assistance policy thresholds, when the patient was eligible for a 100% discount. Hospitals bill patient’s significantly higher prices than Medicare for identical services. 

The photo below does not represent the Medicare price for this bill, but rather the difference of prices for each code if they were billed separately.

Title: Comment from Lee, Leona
Comment: Hello, I've lived in Cook County, IL for over 40 years. I've always had insurance, and lately also Medicare & Medicaid- yet still, there have been several times where I suddenly got a letter from a collections agency or it showed up on my credit report, that I suddenly owe a co-pay for a test or surgery. I've had sudden collection demands for $400, a co-pay from Advocate Christ Hospital for spinal surgery in 2008. Also: several hundred from an X-ray co-pay I did not even know existed, at Illinois Masonic Hospital in Lakeview.  And, $1000 for Occupational Therapy when I had to get a finger amputated in 2020. I have gone to & called the hospital's financial offices, where I was told I HAD to make payments. I am disabled & get SSDI, so I have no wages to garnish. I'm disabled, on a fixed income, and cannot work, yet I was told I HAD to make payments, in one case I paid off half & they STILL sent it to collections!  This is ridiculous. I need dental work, and I need both knees replaced- but I have put things off because I am afraid of medical bills, afraid of anything else going to collections, and I will not be able to find my next apt because these things are on my credit report history!

Title: Comment from American Animal Hospital Association
Comment: Please see attached comments from the American Animal Hospital Association.
Attachment Files_extracted:
9/5/2023 
 
Director Chopra: 
 
I am pleased to respond on behalf of the American Animal Hospital Association (AAHA) to the Request for 
Information Regarding Medical Payment Products (RFI) issued by the Consumer Financial Protection 
Bureau, Department of Health and Human Services, and the Treasury Department.  AAHA is the only 
exclusive companion-animal veterinary association, serving nearly 50,000 individual veterinary providers, 
including veterinarians, technicians, managers, client service specialists, and others.  AAHA is the leader in 
developing benchmarks of excellence, business practice standards, informative publications, and 
educational programs designed to help companion-animal practices thrive. 
 
Although veterinary care is not within scope of the RFI, the agencies’ discussion pertains to popular 
payment methods that are used extensively in veterinary care.  AAHA believes it is important for the 
agencies to understand the perspective of veterinarians and their customers in the event there is an 
attempt to regulate the use of credit for care. 
 
Elective procedures are not common in veterinary medicine.  Pet owner clients come to their veterinary 
care provider for necessary things, such as wellness care or urgent care and they generally pay out of 
pocket for this care, as pet insurance is not yet widely adopted and veterinarians are not in a position to 
administer payment plans.  Unless the client has the ability to pay the invoice up front, the options for 
payment include credit cards (including CareCredit), borrowing from friends/family, or foregoing care for 
the household pet. 
 
For those clients who do not have access to credit, veterinarians frequently administer “economic 
euthanasia.”  This practice is as tragic as it sounds.  When a family does not have the means to pay for 
critical care for a beloved pet, euthanasia is frequently the only humane alternative.  This creates significant 
stress on the family, as well as on the veterinary practice. 
 
Economic euthanasia is traumatic not only for pet owners who feel tremendous guilt, but also for the 
veterinary teams—some of whom then get blamed for not providing free or heavily discounted care.  
Burnout, attrition, and depression are common and the economics of pet care are a factor. 
 
Public policy should expand, not restrict, access to credit for veterinary care.  Products like CareCredit are a 
critical tool for veterinary offices to offer their clients when talking about pet care.  In my over twenty years 
in veterinary medicine, I can say with confidence that CareCredit plays a major role in providing access to 
care that would otherwise be unattainable.  Our members get positive feedback about CareCredit.  
Personally, I cannot tell you how many times, especially in emergency practice, a pet owner would tell me 

 
 
they had CareCredit and I would breathe a sigh of relief knowing the pet would be able to get the care it 
needed. 
 
The RFI also suggests that deferred interest promotional financing is somehow harmful to consumers.  That 
is not the experience our members have.  Our members are not successful when they offer financing 
options that consumers find unattractive or even deceptive.  Conversely, customers are loyal to practices 
that give them a variety of payment options that work with their budgets.  This is why products like 
CareCredit are popular with our members.  While credit is an important facilitator of care, deferred interest 
financing can make it more affordable than other types of credit.  Not only does a pet owner get access to 
pet care with deferred interest financing, but they also can avoid all interest costs if they pay their balance 
within the promotional period.  This is something that most credit cards do not offer, but can make the cost 
of animal care easier to afford.  AAHA strongly urges the agencies to preserve access to deferred interest 
financing. 
 
I recognize the RFI is focused on the care provided by hospitals and how clients may pay for such care.  
However, the agencies should understand that products like CareCredit are beneficial because pet owners 
can use them for a variety of purposes not related to human medical care.  Restrictions on CareCredit and 
similar products could have significant unintended consequences, especially if the utility or value 
associated with the card is diminished. 
 
Thank you for the opportunity to provide AAHA’s perspective.   
 
 
Sincerely, 
 
 
 
Dr. Jessica Vogelsang,  
Chief Medical Officer 
American Animal Hospital Association

Title: Comment from McClung, Nell
Comment: Credit Card Companies such as Credit One had a third party offer disability insurance to insure/pay for the monthly installments if a claim is filed and approved.  However, the third party solicited the insurance coverage when I informed them, I was on Medicare Disability.  That third party charged Credit One since 2010 for disability premiums. I filed a claim in 2019, I was told I did not qualify because I was disabled.  I contacted Credit One and was told they would refund the premiums for the last 10 years.  They only refunded the premiums for maybe the last 5 years.  I requested that they credit the interest charged on those illegally charged premium.  This was a difficult procedure since all credit card companies have customer service centers in India, China or other Third World Countries.  Those agents do not know the intricacies of accounting as it relates to billing.  I was transferred 5 times, spent over an hour on the phone. I sent Faxes and followed up with calls.  I went through the same series with different agents EVERY time I called.  They needed a supervisor.  The Supervisor needs a supervisor.  This is not how America use to do business before technology got involved with Customer Service.  We have complaints about immigration, but Congress allows Corporations to hire immigrants from other Countries to man their Customer Service Centers knowing they are under qualified and cannot speak or understand English well. Consumers just give up because of the red tape. It's a win for Corporations because they have very low labor cost and Consumers incur interest from these disputed claims or purchases. Banks hold on to the money which is making interest on the money which they do not legally own. Credit One refused to send me an itemize list of the disability charges and interest, as well as the contact information for their Third-Party Partner.  Credit One ended up selling my debt to Resurgent in 2020. I contacted them by phone and fax to tell them why I am not paying the bill.  I disputed the balance. Resurgent only sent a copy of the statement from the last time Credit One billed me as proof I owed them the debt.  I disagreed with them and stated that I need the itemized charges and interest related to the disability premiums over those 10 years.  They responded they do not have it.  I responded they have failed to prove I owe the debt.  I have this as a collection item on my credit report.  Banks are refusing to answer these disputes.  CFPB Complaints, which I have submitted, for Credit One and Resurgent, have not made any progress in resolving this issue.  Banks and Corporations are clearly aware that the CFPB does not do anything after a response is sent.  It is a waste of time if you do not implement some type of regulation which requires them to address the issue timely or per the FDCPA Rule regarding ownership of the debt is not established and the Consumer can sue to recover based on that regulation.  This should be across the board for every bank. They affect millions of consumers. Medical Credit Cards are another step in predatory lending and pushed on patients when they are in need of medical assistance.  There should be a limit on the interest rate that can be charged and repayment term.  I suspect that these Medical Credit Cards pays kickbacks to the Medical Facility that offer them.  This is also predatory, and the hardship is on the poor, seniors, disabled and working-class poor. We are living in an era of "Unchecked Capitalism" since the 1990's deregulations of ownership rules and initial regulations barred this type of predatory profiteering and economic discrimination. The 1996 FCC approval of Opinionated Broadcasting/Publishing has made it very difficult to get true information regarding what Consumer are getting.  Congress makes laws for the Corporations and Billionaires and no longer make laws for the betterment of the Citizen/Consumers of America.  This needs to change.
