Fintech and medical expenses: What social workers should 
know
Mathieu Despard , PhD, MSW
a, Sally A. Hageman , PhD, MSW
b, 
and Maudia Ahmad , MSW, LCSW, LCAS-A, CCMc
aDepartment of Social Work, University of North Carolina, Greensboro, North Carolina, United States; 
bSociology, Social Work and Criminology, Idaho State University, Pocatello, Idaho, United States; 
cSociology and Social Work, North Carolina State Agriculture & Technology University, Greensboro, 
North Carolina, United States
ABSTRACT
Individuals who cannot afford out of pocket medical expenses 
may reduce health care use, resulting in poorer health out­
comes. To ease the situation, employers turn to financial tech­
nology (“fintech”) health care credit applications. We examine 
whether an employer-sponsored credit fintech application 
(MedPut) helps employees manage medical expenses. Results 
of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and probit regression mod­
els reveal MedPut users did worse financially and delayed health 
care due to cost more often than employees who did not use 
MedPut. Results may inform social work policy and direct prac­
tice perspectives on fin-tech and medical expenses.
ARTICLE HISTORY 
Received 30 July 2022  
Accepted 8 March 2023 
KEYWORDS 
Medical debt; financial 
technology; out-of-pocket 
costs; medical credit; health 
care use
The United States (U.S.) has a unique system of financing health care in which 
employers, employees, other individuals, and government pay into various 
public and private insurance plans that finance care. Over half of individuals 
have health insurance through their employer. Plans offered by employers vary 
widely concerning employees’ out-of-pocket (OOP) expenses including pre­
miums, co-payments, deductibles, and co-insurance. More than half (59%) of 
all employees in the U.S. are covered by a high deductible health plan (HDHP) 
through their employer (Kaiser Family Foundation, 2022a). These plans are 
attractive to employers because premiums are typically lower than for other 
forms of employer-sponsored insurance. HDHPs require that employees incur 
OOP up to the deductible amount at least $1,400 for an individual and $2,800 
for a family (HealthCare.gov, 2022) before the plan kicks in, unless their 
employer also funds health reimbursement accounts (HRAs) or health savings 
accounts (HSAs).
Lower-paid employees enrolled in HDHPs without employer HRA or HSA 
contributions might find it hard to come up with the cash to pay OOP health 
care expenses until they reach their deductible (Bernstein, 2014). This

challenge is even greater among employees who already have unpaid health­
care bills. Recognizing these challenges, some employers have offered employ­
ees MedPut, a digital financial application that helps employees pay medical 
bills and other health care costs using interest-free payment plans and bill 
negotiation services. The purpose of this study was to examine the registration 
choice, use of, and benefits from using MedPut among employees enrolled in 
HDHPs. Accordingly, our research questions are as follows:
(1) What were the registration and usage rates for MedPut? Did these rates 
vary based on employee and employer characteristics?
(2) What were the frequency, amount, and disposition of health care bills 
submitted for payment? Did bill submission vary based on employee 
characteristics?
(3) What were the experiences of employees in paying for health care? To 
what degree do employees put off health care due to cost concerns? Did 
these experiences differ among MedPut users and non-users?
Findings from this study may inform employer perspectives on health 
insurance and other benefits and policy concerning the intersection of health 
care financing, consumer financial health, and health outcomes. Social work­
ers may advocate to improve employer health insurance policy and health care 
financing options to better protect financially vulnerable populations.
Review of the literature
Prior research draws a connection between individuals’ OOP expenses and 
their health care use (Al Rowas et al., 2017; Brot-Goldberg et al., 2017; 
Wharam et al., 2018). These studies found that individuals are sensitive to 
changes in OOP expenses and reduce health care consumption when these 
expenses increase. Another area of research draws a link between medical debt 
and lessened health care use (Bennett & Dismuke, 2010; Doty et al., 2005; 
Wiltshire et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). When confronted with high deduc­
tibles and existing medical debt, individuals’ reductions in health care use may 
have downstream negative effects; they may avoid preventive care or put off 
receiving care for a chronic illness and risk experiencing a catastrophic and 
expensive care event.
Difficulties affording OOP healthcare expenses may result in accumulating 
medical debt; Himmelstein et al. (2019) found that nearly 60% of individuals 
filing for bankruptcy cited medical expenses as a key reason. Difficulty repay­
ing medical debt may render individuals reluctant to seek additional care 
because it would add to an already unmanageable debt burden (Doty et al.,  
2005).
94
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

Individuals have an array of choices: personal savings, HRAs (if offered by 
their employer), HSAs, Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs), credit cards, 
unsecured bank loans, hospital and health care provider charity care discounts 
and payment plans, and payday loans and similar high-interest rate credit 
products. Yet Seymour (2020) found that most individuals with cancer used 
their personal savings to cover medical bills but were still unable to avoid 
medical debt. Peter et al. (2016) found that individuals who used a HDHP 
paired with an HSA saved more but reduced their use of health care services 
compared to respondents who were enrolled in a full coverage health care 
plan. Kalousova and Burgard (2013) found a positive association between 
credit card debt, medical debt and delaying health care services regardless of 
socioeconomic and health differences. These findings suggest that as debt 
rises, health care use falls.
The focus of our study is on a fintech product meant to help workers in 
HDHPs better afford healthcare. Fintech offers access to new types of financial 
products and services that may benefit individuals and families. Digital and 
mobile payment platforms make it easier for people to conduct transactions 
and send and receive money from family members and friends, personal 
financial management apps make it easier to track spending, and savings 
apps can help people build emergency savings via goal setting, transaction 
round-ups, and automatic saving (Despard, in press). A review of over 60 
studies of fintech showed that the use of fintech is correlated with improve­
ments in spending, loan repayment, and saving behaviors and can improve 
access to credit, yet overall evidence on the benefits of fintech is mixed and it is 
particularly unclear whether fintech benefits lower-income individuals and 
families (Despard, in press). In our study, MedPut may have given workers an 
affordable source of credit to pay overdue medical bills and some relief 
through bill reductions. A fintech credit product like MedPut is especially 
important for workers who have low or no credit scores and cannot access 
affordable credit (Atkinson, 2020; Urban Institute, 2021). In describing results 
from our study of MedPut, we can help shed light on ways in which fintech 
may help workers address problems paying for and utilizing healthcare.
Methods
This study was part of a project called the Financial Solutions Lab (FSL) led by 
the Financial Health Network, a national nonprofit. The Lab tests different 
ways to promote financial health using digital technologies. We partnered with 
the Financial Health Network to study digital financial products and services 
offered through the workplace as part of the 5th cohort of the Lab.
For this study, we examined one of these products, MedPut, a digital 
financial application that helps employees pay medical bills and other health 
care costs using interest-free payment plans and bill negotiation services. The 
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
95

monthly fee to have MedPut is paid by the employer, the employee, or 
a combination of the two. The employer-paid fee is $2 to $6 per month per 
registered employee, depending on the financing limit, which is from $1,000 to 
$3,000. The employee-paid fee is $3 to $7 per month. MedPut focuses on 
employees enrolled in HDHPs who may need help paying for health care 
expenses until they meet their deductible. Employees register to use the 
MedPut app and upload the health care bills they need help paying. MedPut 
pays the bills after trying to negotiate discounts for the employee. Employees 
make monthly payments via payroll deductions. The amount that employees 
can borrow is limited by monthly repayments capped at 5% of gross pay. 
Repayment terms vary from one month to two years.
Data and sample
Data for this study came from two sources. First, administrative data from the 
MedPut platform was de-identified and shared with researchers for analysis. 
Data were included for 4,317 employees in 21 companies and organizations 
who were invited to use MedPut from January 2018 through July 2020. Data 
included employees’ age, salary range, employer size and industry, and health 
care bills submitted for payment. Second, employees who were registered for 
MedPut were invited by MedPut to complete a survey on two occasions, 
including 120 employees who completed a survey in November 2019 and 
104 employees who completed the same survey in June 2020. A total of 35 
employees completed both surveys. Survey questions asked about employees’ 
experiences with medical bills and debt, use of health care, general household 
financial health, and demographic characteristics. Because MedPut provided 
de-identified administrative and survey data to the authors for analysis, the 
Institutional Review Board at a university in the Midwest determined the 
project to be not human subjects research.
Variables
Dependent variables included whether employees registered for the MedPut 
app, whether registered employees used the app at least once, the number, 
amount, and disposition of submitted bills – current, delinquent, or advance 
payment, and whether a discount was negotiated and the amount of this 
discount. These dependent variables were measured using MedPut adminis­
trative data. Independent variables measured from MedPut administrative 
data included age, job tenure, salary, fee type (self, employer, or shared fee), 
employer size, employer industry, and any dependents in the home.
Additional dependent variables were measured from a survey that MedPut 
users completed concerning financial difficulties and use of MedPut. Financial 
difficulty variables included having problems paying medical bills and being 
96
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

contacted by a collections agency within the prior 12 months, and ability to 
cover household expenses. Also, participants were asked whether in the prior 
six months they had lost a job or taken a pay cut due to illness or injury, 
postponed getting medical or dental care, and/or postponed filling 
a prescription medication because they could not afford it.
Analysis
To answer Research Question 1 concerning variation in MedPut registration 
and use, we estimated the marginal effects of and predicted probabilities 
associated with independent variables on the registration choice using probit 
regression models with covariance control, robust standard errors, and 
employer as a cluster variable. Covariates used in probit models included 
age, salary group, any dependents in the home, MedPut fee type, industry, 
and employer size. To answer Research Questions 2 and 3, we used univariate 
descriptive statistics and bivariate analysis including chi square tests for 
categorical and ordinal level independent variables (e.g., salary group, indus­
try) and t-tests and analysis of variance (ANOVA) for age and job tenure.
Results
Sample description
The mean age of employees who had access to MedPut was 42.79 (SD = 12.69). 
Most (73.20%) employees worked for a large company and the most common 
type of industry was white collar. Over a third of employees had salaries below 
$40,000 and had dependents. Out of 4,317 employees who were invited, 1,234 
or 28.5% registered for MedPut which meant they could submit health care 
bills for payment. Among registrants (N = 1,234), 21.31% used MedPut at least 
once during the study period of 2.5 years. Table 1 provides a full description of 
the sample.
Differences in MedPut registration and use
Marginal effects estimated from probit regression predicting MedPut registration 
indicated that employees with dependents were more than three times as likely to 
register for MedPut (54.0%) compared to employees without dependents (13.8%) 
(p < .01). Employees in small companies or organizations were twice as likely to 
register (68.5%) as employees in medium (29.8%) and large (22.5%) companies or 
organizations (p < .001). Other factors that were statistically significant predictors 
of MedPut registration included lesser job tenure, when employees paid the full fee 
for MedPut, and employees working in blue collar industries. Details of the health

Number, disposition, and amount of medical bills submitted
Most employees (82%) who used MedPut did so only once; 15% used it twice, 
and the remaining 3% used it three or more times; employees submitted a total 
of 485 bills through 263 opened MedPut cases. Most cases1 were for delinquent 
accounts (77%), followed by current bills (20%), and advance payments (3%) – 
such as when a provider requires an upfront deposit. Of the cases for delin­
quent accounts, 21% were for accounts in collections, which means the bill had 
been assigned or sold by the health care provider to a third party debt 
collection agency due to nonpayment. Results from regression models indi­
cated that no factors (e.g., age) had a statistically significant relationship with 
total bills submitted or with bill disposition.
The mean amount of medical bills submitted through MedPut was 
$1,378.96 (SD = $1,032.09) with a range of $44 to $4,958 and a median of 
$1,066.60. Results from a regression model predicting bill amount indicated 
that an increase in one year of age was associated with $15 more in submitted 
bills (p < .05), yet no other factors were statistically significant.

Employee health care experiences and financial circumstances
Employees who registered for MedPut and completed a survey (N = 263) gave 
responses concerning their experiences with medical bills, concerns about health 
care costs, and their financial circumstances. MedPut users were worse off 
financially and were more likely to have put off health care due to cost concerns 
compared to employees who registered for but did not use MedPut. Over half 
(52.46%) and nearly half (45.76%) of users said they had problems paying 
a medical bill and were contacted by a collections agency within the prior 12  
months, compared to 18.55% and 19.49% of non-users (p < .001), respectively. 
Users were also more likely to put off medical or dental care (63.33%) and put off 
filling a prescription (26.67%) due to cost concerns compared to 32.79% (p  
< .001) and 11.48% (p < .01) of non-users, respectively. Employee health care

Discussion
This study examined the characteristics and experiences of employees enrolled 
in HDHPs across 21 companies who were offered MedPut, which allows 
employees to borrow money to pay health care bills and repay amounts 
borrowed through automatic payroll deductions. Regarding our first research 
question, having dependents was a significant predictor of both MedPut

registration and use. This finding suggests that employees with family cover­
age may have cash flow challenges in meeting their deductibles, as plan 
benefits for any family member do not begin until the full family deductible 
is met, with certain exceptions like annual physicals. We also found that 
employees in small companies were more likely to register for MedPut. The 
combination of these two findings may reflect that 31% of workers in small 
companies pay greater than 50% of family premiums compared to 7% among 
workers in larger companies (Kaiser Family Foundation, 2022b). Individuals 
are more likely to have medical debt if they have dependents. In addition, if 
premiums are taking up quite a bit of a person’s income, they may experience 
a greater challenge in paying their medical debt.
In answering the second research question, the vast majority of bills sub­
mitted to be paid by MedPut were for delinquent accounts, a fifth of which were 
in collections status. This finding indicates that employees who used MedPut 
faced a double-bind of paying for health care out-of-pocket until they reached 
their deductible and dealing with existing medical debt. That over a third of bills 
were reduced through negotiation with health care providers and collection 
agencies suggests that employees may have more leverage than they realize in 
getting portions of owed amounts forgiven. Accounts in collection were more 
than twice as likely to be reduced than current and delinquent accounts.
Lastly, MedPut appeared to reach a group of employees who struggle with 
paying for health care and are prone to delay seeking health care because of 
cost concerns. This finding is important because prior research indicates that 
individuals with HDHPs – including those with chronic illnesses such as 
cancer – are more likely than those without HDHPs to delay seeking health 
care (Al Rowas et al., 2017; Wharam et al., 2018, Wharam et al., 2019). In turn, 
delayed care is associated with poor health outcomes (Kraft et al., 2009; 
Prentice & Pizer, 2007). Many financial products and services available to 
patients may not relieve them of medical debt or improve healthcare use rates. 
Several researchers demonstrated the negative financial and health outcomes 
patients experienced due to high OOP costs for health care services (Kalousova 
& Burgard, 2013; Peter et al., 2016; Seymour, 2020). Associations between 
medical debt and health care use reveal individuals who have more medical 
debt are more likely to avoid seeking health care services to the detriment of 
their health (Bennett & Dismuke, 2010; Doty et al., 2005; Wiltshire et al., 2016; 
Zhang et al., 2018).
Study strengths and limitations
This study is one of the few to examine the impact of a fintech application 
(MedPut) on employees’ medical expenses. However, several limitations exist. 
First, only one cohort was included in this study and adding more cohorts may 
help to diversify the sample. Second, data was collected twice, however, only 35 
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
101

employees completed both surveys. The low rate of survey completion limits our 
ability to conduct any longitudinal analyses. Future studies could offer an 
incentive to increase the rate of employee participation in the study over time. 
Third, no data on employee health status was collected, which may impact the 
internal validity of the study. Wharam et al. (2018) found that patients suffering 
from a chronic illness (macrovascular diabetes) were more likely to delay seeking 
health care because of costs than those who did not have a chronic illness. 
Similarly, employees in this study who have a chronic illness may be more likely 
to delay seeking health care due to costs than employees who do not have 
a chronic illness. Fourth, we did not have detailed information about employees’ 
health plans. Our results may have been different had we accounted for elements 
of patient cost share such as deductible amounts and coinsurance rates.
Implications for practice, policy and research
Fintech may make it easier for people to access financial products and services 
that better meet their needs than traditional banking. MedPut provided a source 
of affordable credit to workers who may have lacked better options for getting 
caught up on their medical bills. Yet fintech cannot solve the financial problems 
individuals and families face due to low wages, unaffordable housing, healthcare, 
and childcare, and a lack of social protection (Friedline, 2020). For example, 
a popular fintech app in the workplace is earned wage access (EWA), where 
workers can access funds in advance of payday to help pay bills or meet other 
needs (Despard et al., 2020a). Yet EWA would mostly be unnecessary if employ­
ers paid living wages (Despard et al., 2020b) and workers had paid leave, 
universal childcare, affordable healthcare and other social protections.
From an employer perspective, the $2 to $6 monthly fee is a modest cost to 
offer MedPut as a benefit for employees enrolled in HDHPs to pay for 
healthcare while in their deductible periods. Yet we found that MedPut was 
used mostly to pay for overdue medical bills. More impactful ways for employ­
ers to help employees afford healthcare are to offer insurance plans with lower 
deductibles, offer wage-based premiums (i.e., lower-paid employees pay lower 
premiums) and fund employee HSAs for the deductible amount so employees 
can use money in the HSA to pay for care while under the deductible. But 
employers may be unwilling or unable to pay for these more expensive 
solutions, which suggests the need for changes in public policies concerning 
health coverage as we outline below.
That over a third of bills submitted to MedPut were reduced through 
negotiation suggests that hospitals and other health care providers could do 
a better job of offering charity care discounts (Velasquez, 2021) and zero- 
interest payment plans to patients, especially when patients with HDHPs are 
in their deductible periods. Nearly half of U.S. employees are low-wage earners 
whose median hourly wages fall well within income guidelines for most charity 
102
S. A. HAGEMAN ET AL.

care programs (Ross et al., 2020). Yet charity care accounts for less than 2% of 
expenses among hospitals (Zare et al., 2021). ACA provisions under 
Section 501(r)(4) of the Internal Revenue Code could be strengthened with 
respect to nonprofit hospitals’ required reporting on Schedule H of the Form 
990. Social workers in hospital settings are ideally situated to assist screening 
patients for financial assistance eligibility, especially for patients in need of 
specialty care which is harder for uninsured and underinsured patients to 
access (Hall et al., 2013).
A policy option for increasing health insurance affordability among 
employees is to allow employees to opt-in to Affordable Care Act (ACA) 
marketplace plans with subsidies. Currently, if an employer’s plan meets 
ACA affordability and coverage criteria, employees are ineligible for market­
place subsidies. Under the Federal Poverty Level safe harbor rule of the ACA, 
employer-sponsored coverage is considered affordable if at least one plan 
offering does not exceed $103.14 per month in premiums for single coverage 
(Brandon, 2021). It is vital for social workers to advocate to change policies 
that do not allow marketplace subsidies for financially vulnerable populations.
Premiums are only one component of employees’ cost share and 
many employees need family coverage which is much more expensive. 
The inability of employees to opt-in to ACA marketplace subsidies is 
particularly problematic for employees entering or reentering the work­
force who experience lost Medicaid eligibility ($0 premium) and assume 
employer coverage ($500 premium, on average). Social workers can 
advocate for longer-term and less administratively complex policy solu­
tions that allow individuals and families to opt-in to Medicare coverage, 
raising income eligibility thresholds for Medicaid, and making Medicaid 
a federal program only which provides states budgetary relief and 
protection against the volatility of health care expenditures (Brandon,  
2021).
It is important to consider the implications of these types of policy changes 
with respect to the COVID-19 pandemic. Workers in our study already strug­
gling to pay medical bills might have been reluctant to seek care if they had been 
exposed to the coronavirus (especially in customer-facing industries) and/or 
decided to still come to work to avoid losing pay, potentially putting themselves 
and their coworkers at risk. Nearly a third (29%) of workers with household 
income below $40,000 said they went to work with COVID-19 symptoms 
because they could not afford to lose pay (Kaiser Family Foundation, 2022a), 
suggesting the need for social workers to advocate for universal paid sick leave. 
Also, households in which a member lost a job at some point during the 
pandemic were nearly four times more likely to have problems affording medical 
care or prescription drugs compared to households with no job loss (Despard et 
al., 2021). Having the opportunity to opt-in to ACA Marketplace subsidies or 
Medicare would give workers uninterrupted health coverage in the event of job 
SOCIAL WORK IN HEALTH CARE
103

loss. More research on this topic from the social work perspective is needed to 
protect individuals and families struggling to pay for medical expenses.
Conclusion
We illustrate how a well-intended effort to provide a technological solution to 
help employees enrolled in HDHPs pay for their healthcare expenses illumi­
nated problems of underinsurance. Rather than solely focusing on increasing 
the overall rate of insurance, a standard of affordability should be applied that 
includes individuals and families’ full cost share relative to their incomes and 
used to help assess the sufficiency of employer, health care provider, and 
public policies. Social workers are in an ideal position to advocate for these 
health care policy changes to protect financially vulnerable populations. 
Examples of advocacy activities include organizing meetings and educating 
health care staff, patients, and families about patient eligibility for charity care 
and creating petitions to improve the advertisement of charity care.

To whom it may concern: 
 
Thank you for the opportunity to provide input to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 
(CFPB), U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, and U.S. Department of Treasury (the 
“Agencies”) regarding medical payment products (Request for Information published in the 
Federal Register dated July 12, 2023).  
 
The Agencies should be lauded for their examination of the way in which medical payment 
products are being utilized beyond their original purpose for assisting with costs generated from 
out-of-network and elective care. (The RFI defines “medical payment products” as those “being 
administered by financial services companies, who manage the billing and collections process 
for these products and earn revenue through interest and fees”.) 
 
The expanded use of medical payment products imposes an even greater financial burden on 
many patients, limits their creditworthiness, and raises overall healthcare costs. The Agencies 
should ensure full transparency for all medical credit cards, installment loans, and other medical 
payment products that carry interest charges and consumer fees and can cause significant 
increases in cost of care. Further, the Agencies should ensure that consumers have clear 

 
 
 
 
2
information regarding charity care eligibility and preserve their ability to negotiate bills and 
secure adjustments.  
 
We have a crisis of healthcare affordability in the U.S., which is a bigger topic and which a 
myriad of public policy and private market solutions have attempted to address. In the 
meantime, it is necessary for patients to pay for their rapidly rising portion of healthcare costs, 
especially in-network allowed charges such as deductibles and co-pays. The good news is that 
new products have entered the market and created a stronger and more equitable outcome for 
consumers. Innovative private companies such as PayMedix offer consumer-friendly alternatives 
which simplify the medical billing experience, treat all patients equitably and with dignity, and 
eliminate additional costs for patients and family members.

1. Background 
 
U.S. healthcare expenditures, as measured by the annual National Health Expenditure data 
published by CMS, totaled $4.3 trillion in 2021. This represents 18% of U.S. GDP, up from 13% in 

 
 
 
 
3
2000, 9% in 1980 and 5% in 1960.1 While the portion of total U.S. healthcare costs not covered 
by insurance and therefore paid “out of pocket” by patients declined on average from 47% in 
1960 to 10% in 2021, the total dollars owed by patients has grown 5.9% annually over the same 
period, which is nearly twice the rate of U.S economic growth. In 2021, the NHE data shows out 
of pocket spending grew 10.4% to $433.2 billion. 
 
The crisis of healthcare affordability is well documented, and all stakeholders have had to adapt. 
Employers have increasingly shifted rising costs to employees through High Deductible Health 
Plans (HDHPs), higher co-pays and higher monthly premiums. Healthcare providers have 
tightened up claims processing over the years, and as the amounts due from consumers 
continues to rise, they have introduced easier ways for patients to pay (online portals, card on 
file, text-to-pay) and extended payment terms. Unfortunately, the financing options 
implemented by providers at times have hurt consumers’ ability to negotiate bills and amounts 
due, reduced their access to financial assistance/ charity care, and brought in unfavorable 
financing terms (interest charges and fees) that are not transparent to patients. This has further 
reduced access to care. 
 
In 2022, four in ten adults (43%) said that they or a household member put off or postponed 
needed health care due to cost.2 When they do receive care, patients are stressed by paying the 
bill. In a recent study, over one-half of Americans said that in the last six months paying for 
medical bills has been stressful (52%) and that stress has had some impact on their physical or 
mental health (92%).3 
 
Another recent report indicated how widespread the problem has become, with 23.5% of 
middle-class people (nearly 17 million) having medical debt in 2020, which was more than the 
22% of lower-income population.4 Making matters worse, healthcare providers may offer so-
called “in-house payment plans” to patients to pay off their hospital charges over time, and 
then turn around and deny care to patients who have unpaid balances outstanding.5 
 
It is also important to make sure consumers understand their medical bills, and can dispute 
hospital, physician, and other charges when they believe they are incorrect. In one recent study, 
59% of providers rated patient financial literacy as “poor” despite their own investments in 
patient educational materials and financial counselors. 6 A separate study found that 56% of

Americans feel “completely lost” when it comes to understanding their health plans.7 In one 
consumer survey, 38% of patients said their medical bills were somewhat confusing or 
extremely confusing.8 Any solutions to help patients afford healthcare should include help to 
improve patients’ abilities to understand what they owe and advocate for themselves on the 
financial side of healthcare. 
 
This is especially important because medical billing errors occur frequently – half of 
respondents to a recent study reported incorrect charges of at least $200, and a quarter saw a 
difference of more than $500 on their bills.9 When patients use medical payment products, 
including credit cards and installment loans that convert medical debt to consumer debt, they 
often lose the ability to go back and ask for adjustments to incorrectly billed amounts. In fact, 
once a billed amount moves over to a medical payment product, some interpretations hold that 
this represents a consumer's commitment that the charge was justified.10 Effectively once an 
incorrect billed amount is paid on a medical credit card or installment product, some of those 
payment products require the patient to ultimately pay that incorrect amount, even if the 
patient was erroneously overbilled. 
 
There are programs available to help lower-income patients pay their medical bills, including 
each non-profit hospital’s own financial assistance policies and enrollment in state Medicaid 
plans. However, healthcare providers often do a poor job making patients aware of these 
options, and they continue to send bills to patients who could potentially qualify for assistance. 
One study demonstrated that almost half of non-profit hospitals sent medical bills to patients 
whose incomes are low enough to qualify for charity care, accounting for $2.7 billion in medical 
expenses which would have been written off according to the hospital’s own policies if the 
patients had applied for assistance.11  
 
Historically, consumer healthcare costs in the commercial market (i.e., those not in a 
government health care program) have been dealt with in two ways:

1. In-network allowed charges.  These are the copays, deductibles, and out-of-pocket 
amounts for which consumers are responsible under their employer-provided health 
coverage and which received the best-negotiated price with healthcare providers 
o A combination of the provider, insurer, and employer could offer financial

o Assistance was both guaranteed and universal for all consumers regardless their 
individual financial status 
o Consumers have a financial incentive to seek out cost-effective care 
 
2. Out-of-network and elective care.  This is care that consumers choose to consume even 
though it will cost them more money and/or is not a covered medical benefit (e.g., 
cosmetic surgery) 
o If financed, the consumer could individually seek a credit product, or the

Medical payment products as defined in the RFI were traditionally sold for use in the out-of-
network and elective care, and they filled a role in that arena.  More recently, though, these 
medical payment products are being utilized to finance in-network allowed costs after 
insurance.   
 
Many, including PayMedix, view this expansion of medical payment products as problematic.  
Any product for in-network allowed charges that does not apply to all individuals (regardless 
of their creditworthiness) and provide full coverage up to a patient’s out-of-pocket maximum 
is cherry-picking consumers with higher credit ratings.  This approach diminishes universal 
access to care, increases health inequity, and drives up overall healthcare costs.  A better 
approach for assisting consumers with their in-network allowed charges is a “Guaranteed 
Universal Billing and Payment Service.”  We define and explain this term in Section 2.   
 
2. The PayMedix Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service 
 
An alternative model exists that does not place additional financial burden on patients, 
preserves their creditworthiness, and treats all patients equitably and with dignity. This 
Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service is being utilized by a growing number of 

 
 
 
 
6
patients and is addressing the payment challenges faced by patients covered by health plans – 
since 58% of provider bad debt is from patients with health insurance, a 5x increase in 3 years.12 
 
In this model, employers contract with a third party to reimburse healthcare providers for the 
out-of-pocket portion of the in-network medical bill owed by their employees. The third-party, 
for example PayMedix, is then responsible for collecting the out-of-pocket amount owed from 
the patient, who receives the PayMedix SuperEOB combining all billing and EOB information 
from healthcare providers they visit in one consolidated monthly statement.13 Patients see 
immediately what they owe, how to pay it, and how to get credit if they need it in the form of 
extended payment options. The provider is not involved in collecting balances due from these 
patients. 
 
Guaranteed.  Payment is guaranteed to the providers on behalf of all consumers. 
 
Universal.  All employees and their dependents are automatically included, and all in-network 
costs up to the full out-of-pocket maximum can be included.  No one is excluded for any reason. 
 
Billing.  All of the consumer’s medical bills and EOBs are reconciled automatically and are 
replaced by a simple monthly summary statement. 
 
Payment.  Consumers can pay one bill that covers all of the participating providers, and if they 
need to pay over time then they can automatically get a no-fee, 0% interest payment plan. 
 
Service.  Since PayMedix is funded by employers and providers, it is provided at no cost to the 
consumer. 
 
a) Reducing Employee Financial Stress 
 
Employers can play a large role in helping employees reduce the stress of healthcare billing. 60% 
of employees say their employers are responsible for providing financial strategies to help them 
manage expenses, while 72% of corporate benefits managers are interested in a simplified 
medical payments solution, especially payment options that are flexible and offer 0% or low 
interest. 14 Since the PayMedix Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service guarantees 0% 
interest credit for all patients and has no consumer fees, it is an excellent fit for both benefits 
managers and employees.

Employers offer the PayMedix Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service to their 
employees to supplement their commercial group health benefit plan, and provide a benefit to 
employees who have moved to a High Deductible Health Plans (HDHPs). Patients with a HDHP 
incur higher out of pocket medical expenses until they meet their annual deductible. Unlike 
medical payment products that are offered to some patients after they receive care, all patients 
enrolled in PayMedix know before they receive care that they have the flexibility and benefits of 
the PayMedix service. This certainty removes a stubborn and persistent source of a barrier to 
care and allows the patient to proceed with needed medical services. 
 
The characteristics and demographics of participating employees in the PayMedix billing service 
are representative of the full cross section of the employer’s workforce. The billing solution is 
the same for the CEO of the firm as it is for an entry level hire. The PayMedix billing service is 
uniform across the participating workforce of each client. This includes core service features 
such as the configuration of the consolidated bill (“SuperEOB”), and the flexibility of the 
employee to choose the most appropriate bill repayment plan based on their individual 
financial situation.  
 
Since participants know they will be provided care without providers checking to see if they can 
afford it or have outstanding medical debt, PayMedix improves access to care.  The United 
States has seen a recent spike in later-stage disease because consumers were unable or 
unwilling to receive regular care during COVID; people now show up with Stage 4 cancer instead 
of getting caught earlier.  This dynamic has always been the case for those with concerns about 
how they would be able to pay their portion of in-network allowed charges.  PayMedix removes 
that concern – its employer clients have seen annual medical trend in the 2.5-3.4% range for 
years, or about half the market norm.  When people know they can access care, they get more 
care when they need it.  This saves the healthcare system overall as well as improves the health 
status of the individual patients.    
 
b) Health Equity 
 
It is estimated the 43% of Americans avoid seeking care when they need it15 cut back on 
medications to treat chronic conditions like diabetes or fail to follow through on important 
imaging and other tests that can save lives. They do this because they don’t have the means to 
pay for care.  
 
An important feature of the PayMedix model is that unlike medical credit cards and installment 
loans targeted for post-service medical expenses, PayMedix does not require each patient to 
apply or be approved to qualify for the service. There is no review of employee credit history 
during enrollment, and no looking at other data sources that would be used to risk score 
patients and then adjust pricing and other terms at the individual level. Thus, PayMedix does 
not ‘cherry pick’ low-risk patients or seduce high-risk patients with gimmicky teaser rates. When 
an employer selects PayMedix as a benefit, all its employees are offered credit up to their

annual in-network out of pocket maximum with 0% interest and no fees to the patient.  The cost 
of the service is borne by the providers and employers.   
 
While a medical credit card typically offers its revolving line of credit to very few subprime 
consumers (<620 credit score), 25% of PayMedix participants are in the subprime category. This 
includes 10% with a credit score less than 480.  Most of these individuals would not access 
needed care without a Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service like PayMedix, which 
would suboptimize health outcomes and drive up overall healthcare costs since patients will 
end up sicker and ultimately utilize more expensive care.    
 
Medical payment products often find patients at a moment of great weakness – e.g., when they 
find out that they need surgery - without the ability to evaluate options or make a thoughtful 
financial decision. With the PayMedix program already in place, participants need never worry 
about provider-facilitated medical payment product being offered to them when they can least 
afford to make a bad choice. There is no need since PayMedix will have automatically already 
handled the transaction.  This is good for the patient, good for the healthcare provider, and 
good for society at large.  
 
c) Patient Benefits I: Simplified Billing Process 
 
Financial literacy with healthcare billing and insurance remains a chronic and systemic problem 
in the United States, with an estimated two-thirds of Americans unable to correctly define 
healthcare concepts and products like insurance premiums, co-payments, deductibles.16 This 
confusion and lack of knowledge contributes to consumers delaying payment to providers, as 
they are often uncertain if the balance due is fully adjudicated and final. In the PayMedix 
solution, consumers are presented with a simplified, yet comprehensive consolidated bill 
(SuperEOB). This statement clearly summarizes the balance after insurance from all adjudicated 
medical claims, as well as the total amount of payments that have already been made to in-
network providers on the employee’s behalf. This single monthly statement combines the detail 
of an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) with the attributes of a conventional “monthly billing 
statement.”   
 
This simplification is critical.  Today, the healthcare system overall spends a fortune to inundate 
consumers with complicated information that does not reconcile or make sense to most 
consumers.  Those who can and will pay their medical bills do so, but only eventually.  
Experienced consumers know that most of what they receive will not make sense and that they 
should wait until it is all sorted out.  In other words, we as a healthcare system have trained 
consumers to ignore us.   
 
Healthcare cost and quality improvement require consumers to be engaged.  Each provider 
implementing yet another billing or payment point solution could incrementally increase each

9
of their financial results for a time, but the overall increase in the volume of consumer billing 
and payment outreach is driving consumers away.  Think of someone getting surgery – the 
surgeon, the anesthesiologist, radiologist, pathologist, and facility will all bill and ping the 
consumer multiple times with a confusing array of often conflicting information.  Without a 
simple, single Source of Truth like the PayMedix SuperEOB, consumers are left confused, and 
providers are left to spend a fortune on increasingly intrusive and expensive collection efforts 
that still do not generate great yield.  
 
The PayMedix Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service includes making the patient’s 
out of pocket payment to healthcare providers on behalf of the employee. The consumer then 
pays PayMedix for the out-of-pocket amount that has been paid on their behalf. The consumer 
can reimburse PayMedix in full upfront or can create a payment plan to repay PayMedix over 
time. Unlike medical credit cards or installment loan products, repayment plans include no 
interest charges, late fees, hidden costs tied to defined promotional offers or retro-effective 
interest schemes if payment dates are missed. The employee is simply reimbursing for the face 
value of the medical bills already paid on their behalf by PayMedix. There is no cost to the 
employee to participate in the PayMedix solution. This is offered as an employer-subsidized 
program to all employees regardless their salaries, demographics, or credit histories. 
 
d) Patient Benefits II: Reduced Cost and Risk 
 
The PayMedix model does not charge any interest or fees to patients. There are no hidden fees, 
no teaser rates, and no deferred accrued interest. The cost of the PayMedix billing simplification 
service is covered by employers and healthcare providers. Employers pay a fee to offer this 
service to their employees. Providers receive a discounted amount of the post-adjudication 
patient balance and receive guaranteed payment from PayMedix. Patients pay nothing extra, 
and nothing in the PayMedix model contributes to increases in healthcare costs or medical 
inflation.  
 
If a patient needs a payment plan, PayMedix develops a payment plan that works for the 
individual’s particular needs and covers all the costs. This never includes interest charges, late 
fees, origination fees or any other additional expenses for the patient. 
 
PayMedix also supports all employer programs to encourage employees to seek less expensive 
care. This may include, for example, bundled payment programs, zero or low co-pay services at 
more efficient providers, and incentive programs (e.g., healthy living/ wellness or going to 
efficient providers). 
 
e) Provider Benefits I: No Promotional Role 
 
Medical credit cards and installment loans targeted for healthcare expenses are complicated 
financial products. Historically, patients have tended to place a high level of trust in their 
clinicians and their staff. As a result, companies offering medical payment products have often 

 
 
 
 
10 
relied on the provider to introduce and even recommend their products to patients. Promoting 
or at least attempts to guide their patients on selecting the most appropriate medical billing 
oversteps the standard administrative and collections responsibilities of a provider organization. 
It introduces the risk that a patient may receive unwanted or unneeded care that they cannot 
afford.  
 
PayMedix and similar models are not medical payment products and do not involve the 
provider’s staff in driving enrollment or communicating the benefits of participation to patients. 
PayMedix is a simplified billing service offered by employers to all employees, with no 
incentives, inducements, or other features to motivate providers to push patients into 
unnecessary care or extra financing costs. 
 
f) Provider Benefits II: Reduced Administrative Burden 
 
Administrative expenses in the U.S. healthcare system take many forms, but one of the most 
prominent is the process of validating and tabulating amounts owed from providing patient 
care, and then sending bills and collecting amounts due from patients. Hospitals, physician 
offices, surgery centers, labs, imaging centers, and other clinical facilities have invested in 
people and technology tools to accomplish these tasks.  
 
While the adoption of new revenue cycle technology and service solutions by clinical facilities 
has eased some of the burden of processing medical insurance claims, collecting balances owed 
by consumers remains a universal and expensive challenge for healthcare providers. Billing 
teams have invested in portal technologies to enable patient self-service, added payment 
innovations such as “card on file” to securely store credit card information for future payments, 
and started to utilize text messaging and robo-dialing to reach patients with overdue balances. 
Medical billing staff spend an enormous amount of time and money chasing patients with 
outstanding balances, and risk negatively impacting their patient relationships with the use of 
intrusive communication tools. 
 
The PayMedix model reduces the administrative strain and costs of patient collections for an 
individual provider and avoids damaging the provider-patient relationship. In exchange for a 
modest fee to participate in the PayMedix simplified billing solution, providers avoid incurring 
the costs associated with collecting overdue balance from patients participating in the PayMedix 
model. Providers send fewer billing statements and make fewer phone calls and are able to use 
their resources to focus on clinical care and ensure the best possible patient outcomes. 
Providers also appreciate that the PayMedix model is a non-recourse arrangement, so they have 
certainty of payment with no risk that non-performing patient accounts will “boomerang” back 
to their balance sheet.  
 
Furthermore, the PayMedix model helps to reduce the cost of patient collections across all 
participating providers. PayMedix centralizes patient collection activities in one organization and 
operates a compassionate, efficient collection process at scale. This lowers the unit cost of 

 
 
 
 
11 
overall patient collections below what each provider would achieve doing these activities on 
their own. 
 
g) Charity Care 
 
Unlike medical credit cards and installment loans targeted for medical expenses, providers 
assume no role in enrolling patients in the PayMedix service. Participating patients are enrolled 
in PayMedix through their employers. The providers play no role in introducing or incenting 
patients to participate in the PayMedix service.  
 
This means providers have no conflicted interests when identifying and helping their patients 
access other local, regional, or federal financial assistance programs. Importantly, the financial 
assistance process is not hindered by the patient’s participation in the PayMedix billing solution 
service. Furthermore, PayMedix helps consumers gather the necessary information and apply 
for financial assistance from healthcare providers. As of July 2023, PayMedix has assisted 
patients in qualifying for $750,000 of charity care for over 5,000 individual medical claims. This 
advocacy offered by PayMedix is communicated on the firm’s SuperEOB and elsewhere, and in 
these cases -- and with the consumer's permission -- PayMedix can assemble documents and 
aggregate key qualifying information to the health system on behalf of and with no fee to the 
consumer. PayMedix is not aware of examples in which medical credit cards and installment 
loan vendors intervene for their customers to identify charity care relief. 
 
h) Disputed Medical Bills  
With the PayMedix billing simplification service, patients can always challenge a bill. The 
PayMedix customer service team will support the patient in understanding and ultimately 
disputing any bill with which they disagree. Most customer disputes center on whether the 
claims should have been paid in the first place. Determining the correct answer to this question 
is the responsibility of the health plan. PayMedix will work with the health plan and the 
consumer if the consumer believes a claim should not have been paid and their out-of-pocket 
amount should have been $0. If the consumer is correct, then PayMedix will process any 
adjustments and refund the appropriate amount of money to the consumer.

While the three major U.S. credit reporting companies recently agreed to remove medical debt 
of less than $500 from credit reports, this only begins to address the way these balances affect 
patient credit. According to the U.S. Census, in 2017, 19% of U.S. households carried medical 
debt and the median amount owed was $2,000.17 One source estimates that most Americans

the costs and benefits for health care providers of offering each of these methods? Are there 
situations where one method of payment is more advantageous than another?  
 
Historically, consumer healthcare costs in the commercial market (i.e., those not in a 
government health care program) have been dealt with in two ways: 
 
- 
In-network allowed charges.  These are the co-pays, deductibles, and out-of-pocket 
amounts for which consumers are responsible under their employer-provided health 
coverage and which received the best-negotiated price with providers 
o A combination of the provider, insurer, and employer could offer financial 
assistance to consumers in order to pay their portion (e.g., payment plans, 
HRAs/FSAs/HSAs, health/wellness incentive accounts, etc.) 
o Assistance was both guaranteed and universal for all consumers regardless their 
individual financial status 
o Consumers had a financial incentive to seek out cost-effective care 
 
- 
Out-of-network and elective care.  This is care that consumers choose to consume even 
though it will cost them more money and/or is not a covered medical benefit (e.g., 
cosmetic surgery) 
o If financed, the consumer could individually seek a credit product, or the provider 
could partner with a financial services firm to offer that function 
o Consumers are individually underwritten, and whether credit is offered and if so 
on what terms varies significantly 
o Consumer interest and fees are typically a key component of these products 
 
In the RFI, the Agencies defined medical payment products as those “being administered by 
financial services companies, who manage the billing and collections process for these 
products and earn revenue through interest and fees.”  These were the types of products that 
traditionally were sold for use in the out-of-network and elective care, and they filled a role in 
that arena.  More recently, though, these medical payment products have been brought to 
finance the in-network costs after insurance.   
 

 
 
 
 
24 
Many, including PayMedix, view this expansion of medical payment products as problematic.  
Any product for in-network allowed charges that does not apply to all individuals (regardless 
of their creditworthiness) and provide full coverage up to a patient’s out-of-pocket maximum 
is cherry-picking consumers with higher credit ratings.  This approach diminishes universal 
access to care, increases health inequity, and drives up overall healthcare costs.  A better 
approach for assisting consumers with their in-network allowed charges is a “Guaranteed 
Universal Billing and Payment Service”.  We define and explain this term below.  
 
This Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service is being utilized by a growing number of 
patients and is addressing the payment challenges faced by patients covered by health plans – 
since 58% of provider bad debt is from patients with health insurance, a 5x increase in 3 years.20 
In this model, employers contract with a third party to reimburse healthcare providers for the 
out-of-pocket portion of the in-network bill owed by their employees after they receive medical 
care. The third-party, for example PayMedix, is then responsible for collecting the out-of-pocket 
amount owed from the patient, who receives the PayMedix SuperEOB combining all billing and 
EOB information from healthcare providers they visit in one easy-to-read, consolidated monthly 
statement21. Patients see immediately what they owe, how to pay it, and how to get credit if 
they need it in the form of extended payment options. The provider is not involved in collecting 
balances due from these patients.  This alternative model does not place additional financial 
burden on patients, preserves their creditworthiness, and treats all patients equitably and with 
dignity. 
 
A breakdown of the components is as follows: 
 
Guaranteed.  Full payment is guaranteed to the providers on behalf of all consumers 
 
Universal.   All employees and their dependents are automatically included, and all in-network 
costs up to the full out-of-pocket maximum can be included.  No one is excluded for any reason. 
 
Billing.  All of the consumer’s provider bills and EOBs are reconciled automatically and are 
replaced by a simple monthly summary statement 
 
Payment.  Consumers can pay one bill that covers all of the participating providers, and if they 
need to pay over time then they can automatically get a no-fee, 0% interest payment plan 
 
Service.  Since PayMedix is funded by employers and providers, it is provided at no cost to the 
consumers

chc2305-001a.pdf.

The Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service approach also helps drive overall system 
efficiency.  Administrative expenses in the U.S. healthcare system take many forms, but one of 
the most prominent is the process of validating and tabulating amounts owed from providing 
patient care, and then sending bills and collecting amounts due from patients. Hospitals, 
physician offices, surgery centers, labs, imaging centers, and other clinical facilities have 
invested in people and technology tools to accomplish these tasks.  
 
While the adoption of new revenue cycle technology and service solutions by clinical facilities 
has eased some of the burden of processing medical insurance claims, collecting balances owed 
by consumers remains a universal and expensive challenge for healthcare providers. Billing 
teams have invested in portal technologies to enable patient self-service, added payment 
innovations such as “card on file” to securely store credit card information for future payments, 
and started to utilize text messaging and robo-dialing to reach patients with overdue balances. 
Medical billing staff spend an enormous amount of time and money chasing patients with 
outstanding balances, and risk negatively impacting their patient relationships with the use of 
intrusive communication tools. 
 
The PayMedix Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service reduces the administrative 
strain and costs of patient collections for an individual healthcare provider and avoids damaging 
the provider-patient relationship. In exchange for a modest fee to participate in the PayMedix 
simplified billing solution, providers avoid incurring the costs associated with collecting overdue 
balance from patients participating in the PayMedix model. Providers send fewer billing 
statements, make fewer phone calls, and are able to use their resources to focus on clinical care 
and ensure the best possible patient outcomes. Providers also appreciate that the PayMedix 
model is a non-recourse arrangement, so they have certainty of payment with no risk that non-
performing patient accounts will “boomerang” back to their balance sheet.  
 
The PayMedix model helps to reduce the cost of patient collections across all participating 
providers. PayMedix centralizes patient collection activities in one organization and operates a 
compassionate, efficient collection process at scale. This lowers the unit cost of overall patient 
collections below what each provider would achieve doing these activities on their own.

b) CFPB-Specific Questions  
 
1. What actions should the CFPB consider taking to address problematic practices related to 
medical credit cards or loans, including debt collection and credit reporting practices?  
 
The Agencies should be lauded for their examination of the expanded use of medical payment 
products that impose an even greater financial burden on many patients, limit their 
creditworthiness, and raise overall healthcare costs. CFPB in particular should ensure full 
transparency for all medical credit cards, installment loans, and other medical payment 
products that carry interest charges and consumer fees and can cause significant increases in 
cost of care. Further, the Agencies should ensure that consumers have clear information 
regarding charity care eligibility and preserve their ability to negotiate bills and secure 
adjustments.  
 
We strongly urge the Agencies to consider the positive benefits of alternative offerings to 
traditional medical payment products. One such model that resolves many of the legitimate 
regulatory concerns of the medical credit card and installment loan category is the Guaranteed 
Universal Billing and Payment Service offered as an employer solution.  
 
Suggested actions: 
1. Encourage U.S. employers to play a bigger role in helping employees get access to the 
care they need, including improved ways to pay for the patient’s share of medical 
charges 
a. Provide tax advantages for offering Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment 
Services to employees 
2. Clarify the definition of medical payment products to include the word “limited” 
a. Medical payment products is defined in the RFI as those “being administered by 
financial services companies, who manage the billing and collections process for 
these products and earn revenue through interest and fees”.  We would suggest 
adding the word “limited” before “products” in this definition since many medical 
payment products are limited to certain individuals (e.g., those with higher credit 
scores), limited in duration (e.g., 12 months), and/or limited by a dollar cap (e.g., 
$2000) 
i. Any limitations like those above should be fully and prominently disclosed 
to consumers, providers, and employers 
b. Consider further differentiating between financial products designed for limited 
or focused use for out-of-network or elective services and solutions designed to 
address consumer amounts due for in-network allowed charges after insurance 

 
 
 
 
33 
i. For example, this could be as simple as not allowing any product with 
limitations such as those listed in 1.a above to be used for in-network 
allowed charges that go through insurance first 
ii. Similarly, products that charge consumers interest or fees for in-network 
allowed charges could be disallowed 
3. Focus federal rulemaking on simplifying information provided to consumers. 
a. It would be easy but counter-productive to significantly expand claim-by-claim 
disclosures to consumers. In healthcare, consumers are already overwhelmed and 
end up tuning it all out 
b. Instead, focus on consumers not transactions. 
i. For example, allow consumers to set their preferences for communications 
1. For investment accounts (e.g., at Schwab or Fidelity), consumers 
can elect to consolidate mandated reporting, assign access and 
reporting to a spouse, elect electronic not paper delivery, etc. 
2. Once preferences are set by the consumer, their agent can execute 
their instructions on their behalf 
ii. Review the intersection of state and federal disclosure requirements. 
1. Conflicting or overlapping requirements force market participants 
to conservatively include absolutely anything that could be 
required somewhere.  This drives more cost as well as consumer 
confusion 
a. It is far better to make sure consumers regularly know the 
top 5 things that will likely impact them than repeatedly try 
to tell them the 50 things that might possibly matter very 
occasionally.  Consumers may well take action based on 
the former, but will tune out and miss the key takeaways 
from the latter 
4. Make it clear that any debt that is the result of in-network, post-insurance medical costs 
should still be considered medical debt regardless of what product financed it.  All the 
rules regarding medical debt should still apply in this case

1. What actions should HHS consider taking to address problematic practices related to

PayMedix does not offer a medical payment product, as defined in the RFI, and cannot comment

therefore does not have experience with Medicare, Medicaid, or the Children’s Health Insurance 
Program, or patients enrolled in Affordable Care Act Marketplace plans.

Nevertheless, it is important to note that HHS has recently issued initial guidance regarding the 
implementation of “smoothing” for Medicare drug plans.24  In effect, health plans must offer 
financing for drug costs to their Medicare members who have drug coverage, and that financing 
must go all the way to the $2,000 individual cap.  All members are to be eligible for this 
program, and no interest or consumer fees are to be charged. 
 
Conceptually, this is similar to what the PayMedix Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment 
Service has been doing for 15 years.  PayMedix has transacted $5 billion through this 
mechanism, has very high client retention and consumer satisfaction, and has reduced financial 
barriers to care.   
 
As implementation of the new Medicare drug cost “smoothing” program proceeds, HHS should

which may not. Health plans and consumers will need clear guidance.   
 
2. What types of health insurance (Medicare, Medicaid, private insurance, etc.) are

The No Surprises Act and its surprise billing protections have not affected the PayMedix

The question regarding consumers’ financial obligations – what percentage of that total cost

In the PayMedix Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service, employers contract with

41 
d) Treasury-Specific Questions  
 
1. What policy actions should Treasury consider taking to address problematic practices 
related to medical credit cards or loans, including debt collection and credit reporting

42

delivers community benefits or provides financial assistance, including in their filings of Form

No response provided. 
 
8. Does the availability of medical payment products generally benefit the community or 
assist patients financially?  
 
PayMedix does not offer a medical payment product, as defined in the RFI, and cannot comment 
on that model.  More generally, however, PayMedix can offer the following perspective and 
potential actions regarding the broader market: 
 
Historically, consumer healthcare costs in the commercial market (i.e., those not in a 
government health care program) have been dealt with in two ways: 
 
- 
In-network allowed charges.  These are the co-pays, deductibles, and out-of-pocket 
amounts for which consumers are responsible under their employer-provided health 
coverage and which received the best-negotiated price with providers 
o A combination of the provider, insurer, and employer could offer financial 
assistance to consumers in order to pay their portion (e.g., payment plans, 
HRAs/FSAs/HSAs, health/wellness incentive accounts, etc.) 
o Assistance was both guaranteed and universal for all consumers regardless their 
individual financial status 
o Consumers had a financial incentive to seek out cost-effective care 
 
- 
Out-of-network and elective care.  This is care that consumers choose to consume even 
though it will cost them more money and/or is not a covered medical benefit (e.g., 
cosmetic surgery) 
o If financed, the consumer could individually seek a credit product, or the provider 
could partner with a financial services firm to offer that function 
o Consumers are individually underwritten, and whether credit is offered and if so 
on what terms vary significantly 
o Consumer interest and fees are typically a key component of these products 
 
In the RFI, the Agencies defined medical payment products as those “being administered by 
financial services companies, who manage the billing and collections process for these 
products and earn revenue through interest and fees.”  These were the types of products that 
traditionally were sold for use in the out-of-network and elective care, and they filled a role in 
that arena.  More recently, though, these medical payment products have been brought to 
finance the in-network costs after insurance.   
 

 
 
 
 
43 
Many, including PayMedix, view this expansion of medical payment products as problematic.  
Any product for in-network allowed charges that does not apply to all individuals (regardless 
of their creditworthiness) and provide full coverage up to a patient’s out-of-pocket maximum 
is cherry-picking consumers.  This approach diminishes universal access to care, increases 
health inequity, and drives up overall healthcare costs.  A better approach for assisting 
consumers with their in-network allowed charges is a “Guaranteed Universal Billing and 
Payment Service”.  We define and explain this term below.  
 
This Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service is being utilized by a growing number of 
patients and is addressing the payment challenges faced by patients covered by health plans – 
since 58% of provider bad debt is from patients with health insurance, a 5x increase in 3 years.25 
In this model, employers contract with a third party to reimburse healthcare providers for the 
out-of-pocket portion of the in-network bill owed by their employees after they receive medical 
care. The third-party, for example PayMedix, is then responsible for collecting the out-of-pocket 
amount owed from the patient, who receives the PayMedix SuperEOB combining all billing and 
EOB information from healthcare providers they visit in one easy-to-read, consolidated monthly 
statement26. Patients see immediately what they owe, how to pay it, and how to get credit if 
they need it in the form of extended payment options. The provider is not involved in collecting 
balances due from these patients.  This alternative model does not place additional financial 
burden on patients, preserves their creditworthiness, and treats all patients equitably and with 
dignity. 
 
A breakdown of the components is as follows: 
 
Guaranteed.  Payment is guaranteed to the providers on behalf of all consumers. 
 
Universal.  All employees and their dependents are automatically included, and all in-network 
costs up to the full out-of-pocket maximum can be included.  No one is excluded for any reason. 
 
Billing.  All of the consumer’s medical bills and EOBs are reconciled automatically and are 
replaced by a simple monthly summary statement. 
 
Payment.  Consumers can pay one bill that covers all of the participating providers, and if they 
need to pay over time then they can automatically get a no-fee, 0% interest payment plan. 
 
Service.  Since PayMedix is funded by employers and providers, it is provided at no cost to the 
consumer.

/media/crowe/llp/widen-media-files-folder/h/hospital-collection-rates-for-self-pay-patient-accounts-report-
chc2305-001a.pdf.  
26 Employees are able to use the Guaranteed Universal Billing and Payment Service for medical claims generated 
with healthcare providers who are in-network for their health plan, and also in the PayMedix network.  

 
 
 
 
44 
The PayMedix service brings multiple benefits to the patient, including a monthly billing

Employees have no incentive or encouragement to use more expensive providers or consume

For the consumer portion due from in-network allowed charges after insurance, PayMedix does

PayMedix does believe that medical payment products have been successfully used for out-of-
network care, elective services, and non-medical coverage (e.g., Lasik or dental care).  Having 
them properly continue in that arena could be appropriate.   
 
4. Conclusion 
 
We strongly urge that the CFPB, HHS and Department of Treasury consider the positive benefits 
of alternative offerings to traditional medical payment products for the consumer portion of in-
network allowed charges after insurance. One such model that resolves many of the legitimate 
regulatory concerns of the medical credit card and installment loan category is the Guaranteed 
Universal Billing and Payment Service offered as an employer benefit solution. PayMedix is an 
example of a vendor operating in this emerging category. PayMedix’s model is attractive for 
each of the stakeholders served. 
 
The PayMedix service brings multiple benefits to the patient, including a monthly billing 
statement that displays all medical charges for the participant and family members. PayMedix’s 
service includes zero interest or fees due from the patient and offers payment plans customized 
to the unique circumstances of the family, which helps patients have lower cost of care. 
Employees have no incentive or encouragement to use more expensive providers or consume 
unnecessary care outside of their in-network providers. PayMedix is offered to all employees 
equally, provides an opportunity to dispute and correct erroneous charges, and supports 
patients in applying for financial assistance. The PayMedix service helps all consumers afford the 
care they need, thus helping improve their physical and mental health. 
 
From the perspective of providers, the PayMedix model provides prompt pay for all in-network 
patient balances for each participant opted into the billing simplification service through their 
employer.  The provider can focus on clinical and other operational responsibilities of running 

 
 
 
 
45 
their practice. They do not promote the PayMedix model to patients, nor does it interfere with 
charity care or financial assistance programs.  
 
For the employer, the PayMedix model provides to employees a product that simplifies paying 
for in-network, out-of-pocket medical expenses for its workforce and reduces the stress and 
confusion that can further contribute to employee physical health issues.  In addition, PayMedix 
employer clients have seen annual medical trend increases of 2.5-3.4% -- about half what the 
market has experienced overall.  When people can get the care they need it when they need it,

them and their employers money.   
 
Out of pocket healthcare costs present a significant and urgent issue for many Americans. While 
traditional medical credit cards and installment loans are widely used today, they also present 
several issues for consumers when used for care traditionally financed by employers, insurers, 
and providers.  It is important that the Agencies recognize alternative, innovative models to 
lower overall medical billing costs and to ensure that all consumers can afford the care they

PayMedix understands that our model is unusual and innovative.  We would be happy to meet
