RE:
Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products
To whom it may concern:
PayZen is pleased to have the opportunity to respond to the Request for Information Regarding Medical
Payment Products (“RFI”) published by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (“CFPB”), Centers for
Medicare & Medicaid, Department of Health & Human Services (“HHS”), and Department of Treasury
(“Treasury”)(collectively the “Agencies”) on July 12, 2023. PayZen appreciates the Agencies’ focus on
this important area and encourages the Agencies to continue using their authority to promote the
development of patient-friendly solutions to the healthcare affordability crisis.
I.
Background on PayZen
PayZen was founded in 2019 with a simple yet critically important mission: to use technology to address
the healthcare affordability problem in a way that benefits both patients and healthcare providers. As the
RFI suggests, healthcare affordability is a significant problem in our country, with both the absolute cost of
healthcare and patients’ share of that cost rising faster than patients’ income or assets. Compounding
this problem, patients are often unaware of their out-of-pocket financial obligations, the options that might
exist to help them satisfy these obligations, or the risks presented by many of those options. On their
part, providers often face shrinking margins and reduced administrative support at a time of increasing
administrative burdens associated with administering financial assistance policies and collecting patient
balances.
1

We estimate that providers can collect approximately 25% of patient balances, with the amount of bad
debt increasing over time. Faced with the need to convert accounts receivable into revenue more
successfully, providers have increasingly turned to a suboptimal solution: consumer finance providers
willing to loan consumers the amount necessary to satisfy their out-of-pocket obligations. While these
credit products may satisfy providers’ short-term interest in collecting funds, they result in patients taking
on even more costly obligations to pay interest and fees — obligations they often do not fully understand
and which only worsen their overall financial health. In addition to the immediate impact on their wallet,
patients who finance their out-of-pocket obligations, in effect, convert their medical debt to general
consumer debt and, therefore, lose important consumer protections, including those protecting them from
negative credit reporting. The end result is bad for patients and, ultimately, providers, as patients are often
forced to defer necessary treatment and will associate the provider with their negative financial
experiences.
PayZen is seeking to use technology to solve this problem. First, PayZen’s solution integrates with
providers’ electronic health record systems and payment portals, which allows for full automation of the
process and facilitates patients’ understanding of their payment obligations and options, including the
potential availability of financial assistance. Second, PayZen utilizes unique datasets and a proprietary
AI-powered underwriting model to design a patient-specific payment plan that breaks patients’ financial
obligation into affordable monthly payments. Most significantly, and in contrast to many of the lenders in
this space, PayZen is able to offer this product to patients without interest or fees. By designing a
payment plan tailored to each patient’s circumstances, PayZen is able to offer providers the ability to
accelerate revenue in a patient-friendly manner that maximizes providers’ yield on their accounts
receivable while reducing providers’ administrative burdens. In effect, by automating processes and
utilizing sophisticated machine learning, PayZen is able to find the most affordable payment plan for each
patient—i.e., the result that optimizes outcomes for both parties to the underlying transaction while
eliminating the negative outcomes that neither party wants (additional interest and fees for the patient,
default, aggressive debt collection tactics, deferred care, etc.).
PayZen currently offers both post-care payment plans and a physical and virtual debit Care Card that can
be used to pay for existing balances or upcoming procedures. As with the post-care plan, Care Card
purchases are immediately split into affordable monthly payments with no interest or fees. In the future,
PayZen intends to offer products that further address challenges in the healthcare affordability segment,
including automation of Medicaid and financial assistance processes, as well as overall patient
affordability triage.
PayZen was founded on the idea that technology can be used to reduce complexity and friction in the
patient payment environment, benefitting both patients and providers. PayZen encourages the Agencies
to use their authorities to promote a marketplace where patients can obtain the care they need without
worrying about whether they will be able to afford it and where providers can focus their resources on
providing that care.
II.
Response to Specific Questions
PayZen offers the following responses to select questions posed by the RFI.
a.
General Questions
2

1.
What are the benefits, costs, and risks of medical payment products for consumers, health care
providers, and companies offering these products?
The benefits, costs, and risks of medical payment products will depend on the product at issue. For
example, consumers who take out high-interest loans to pay their out-of-pocket expenses will significantly
increase the amount they pay for their care. A default on the loan will lead to further negative
repercussions, including, potentially, aggressive debt collection and negative consumer reporting.
Unfortunately, medical debt is the #1 reason for personal bankruptcy. This system allows providers to get
paid for a transaction up-front, but the harm to their patients will ultimately damage their business as
patients are unable to afford subsequent care and may associate the provider with negative financial
outcomes. In the long run, only the provider of the credit product benefits.
PayZen’s product changes the calculus. Patients are able to swap their immediate obligation for an
interest-free payment plan with the provider that is specifically tailored to each patient’s circumstances.
PayZen’s payment options are often more flexible than what providers can offer in-house, and as a result,
more patients are able to access and pay for their care, and fewer patient accounts are placed with
collections agencies. At the same time, providers are able to increase the number of patients that receive
and pay for care, rather than end up in bad debt, without paying any fees for integration or ongoing
services. PayZen accelerates payment to providers while fully assuming the risk of patient default, freeing
up resources and capital for providers to invest in their core business of providing care.
2.
What are the terms of medical payment products, including interest rates and fees?
We note that Appendix A to CFPB’s report on Medical Credit Cards and Financing Plans issued on May
4, 2023 provides a useful summary of many of the medical payment products, including interest rates and
fees, available in the market in 2023.1 Likewise, the National Consumer Law Center published a report
entitled “Health Care Plastic, The Risks of Medical Credit Cards,” that studies one form of medical
payment product, with some additional information on such products.2
The PayZen payment plan allows patients to pay their out-of-pocket obligations over time (up to 60
months) through affordable monthly payments. It does not charge interest or fees.
4.
How concentrated is the medical payment product market, and what role do private equity firms
play in this market?
In our experience, the market for medical payment products is relatively fragmented. As a venture
capital-backed company, PayZen appreciates its investors’ commitment to helping bring to market an
innovative, patient-friendly medical payment product as part of an end-to-end solution focused on patient
affordability, with a focus on long-term outcomes that, if successful, will reward the investors while doing
right by patients and providers.
7.
Patients can pay for care in many different ways, such as by medical credit card or loan, general
purpose credit card, insurance, or through a zero-interest payment plan. What are the costs and
2 See https://www.nclc.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/Report_Health-Care-Plastic.pdf.
1 See https://files.consumerfinance.gov/f/documents/cfpb_medical-credit-cards-and-financing-plans_2023-05.pdf.
3

benefits for health care providers of offering each of these methods? Are there situations in
which one method is more advantageous than another?
Generally speaking, providers whose customers pay their out-of-pocket obligations using a traditional
credit product receive full payment up front, but they may suffer long-term consequences associated with
poor outcomes for their patients’ personal finances. In our experience, providers of elective procedures
are more likely to require up-front payment, in full, which often entails patients’ reliance on a traditional
credit product. Non-elective providers generally receive a portion of their payment from insurance
providers and are therefore focused on the patient’s obligations post-insurance, such as deductibles,
co-payments, and costs of services not covered by insurance. These providers will often offer a no
interest payment plan, but these plans are not tailored to consumers’ individual circumstances and are
therefore unlikely to optimize yield to the same extent as PayZen’s product. Administration of these
products also entails considerable expense and burden, which PayZen assumes for providers.
8.
What incentives do financial services companies offer health care providers, including
revenue-sharing or other financial or non-financial incentives?
We cannot speak to what incentives others in the market offer providers, although we are aware that
some do provide various forms of incentives. However, PayZen does not offer financial incentives to
providers to induce them to do business with PayZen and believes that such financial incentives could
cause providers to recommend products to patients that are not in the patients’ best interests. Rather, we
focus on creating value for patients and providers. PayZen’s products and services allow providers to
accelerate revenue collection and avoid default risk in a manner that is patient friendly. PayZen’s
products and services also relieve providers of significant administrative burden and expense associated
with administering and collecting on payment plans on their own. Accordingly, PayZen encourages the
Agencies to closely scrutinize any incentive payments offered by other medical payment providers and
consider banning any such financial incentives or, at a minimum, requiring that they be clearly,
conspicuously and completely disclosed to patients.
12. To what extent are patients using medical payment products to pay bills that are incorrect, or that
could be covered or defrayed by lower-cost alternatives?
Patients who finance out-of-pocket medical obligations take on an obligation that is generally independent
of their original obligation to the provider. As a result, disputes with a bill, including the amount of
insurance coverage, can be more difficult to resolve. For example, the most frequent reasons for
incorrect billing that we observe are additional coverage/payments after the billing statement has been
sent, or delayed recognition that a patient qualifies for financial assistance. Moreover, to the extent that
such forms of credit become subject to credit reporting, that would compound the challenge of error
resolution.
PayZen’s integration with provider systems ensures that patients can dispute bills in the same way that
they would if their obligation remained with the provider. If the provider determines that a patient owes
less because of additional insurance payments or financial assistance, PayZen will reverse the
enrollment, refund any payments, and, if appropriate, provide a revised payment plan. PayZen is working
on a tool that will use its integration with providers’ systems to automatically determine eligibility for
financial assistance in advance of patients incurring expenses.
4

13. What actions should agencies consider taking to better understand the effects of medical
payment products on consumers and the health care industry, educate consumers and providers
about the risks of these products, and collect complaints?
We recommend that the Agencies review and set standards for: (1) clear and conspicuous disclosures of
all material terms, conditions, risks and benefits of a product during the enrollment process, taking into
account the unique factual context; (2) the integration of payment product providers’ systems with health
care providers’ systems in order to facilitate consumer understanding and the resolution of billing
disputes; and (3) clear disclosure of payment product providers’ collection practices, including consumer
reporting, and adherence to all standards applicable to medical debt.
b.
CFPB-Specific Questions
4.
Do consumers understand the risks of paying medical bills via a medical credit card, installment
loan, or other commercial payment product, including lowered ability to negotiate the bill with their
provider?
Too often, patients do not understand the basic terms of traditional credit products that are offered by
providers to cover patients’ out-of-pocket obligations, including the significant interest and fees that could
accompany those products. Patients also may not understand the secondary risks that come with such
products, including the impact of the product on patients’ consumer credit profile and the risks of
aggressive debt collection practices.
PayZen encourages the Agencies to mandate clear disclosures to consumers of their obligations when
using a traditional credit product, including the impact of fees and interest payments that accompany such
products. The PayZen payment plans utilized by providers do not charge consumers interest or fees and
allow consumers to satisfy their obligations through fixed monthly payments. PayZen also encourages
the Agencies to educate patients on the options that might be available to make healthcare more
affordable, including financial assistance or zero interest payment plans, like those offered by the
providers that PayZen supports.
5.
To what extent are alleged debts placed on medical credit cards and loans sent to debt
collectors? How do medical payment product companies’ debt collection practices differ from
those of health care providers, and are any issuer or provider debt collection practices posing
risks to consumers?
PayZen does not contract with third-party debt collectors or engage in aggressive debt collection
practices. If a patient experiences a hardship that affects their ability to make their monthly payment, they
are encouraged to contact PayZen’s customer support team to review available options, including a
revised payment plan.
6.
How can the CFPB use its authorities to ensure people with medical bills in collections, including
medical payment product debt, are screened for eligibility for financial assistance and other
benefits?
5

The CFPB has broad authority to ensure that patients fully understand the “costs, benefits, and risks
associated with [consumer financial products or services], in light of the facts and circumstances.”3
Because the availability of financial assistance will necessarily impact the amount of any additional debt,
the CFPB can mandate disclosure of whether financial assistance is available and the amount of any
such financial assistance.
c.
HHS-Specific Questions
7.
How might HHS improve patient understanding of options for covering the cost of medical
treatments? At what points in the care process could patients be provided with information about
their financial obligations and payment options?
Patients should be provided with information about their financial obligations and payment options before
treatment, unless providing such information in advance of treatment is impossible. Such information
should include eligibility for financial assistance, as well as the availability of different payment options,
including interest-free options. Patients should receive this information sufficiently far in advance of any
decision on treatment to allow them to make an informed judgment regarding their treatment and its
impacts on their finances.
d.
Treasury-Specific Questions
8.
Does the availability of medical payment products generally benefit the community or assist
patients financially?
It depends. As patient payment responsibility increases, achieving equitable access to healthcare will
depend on the availability of patient-friendly payment products, such as no-interest payment plans, and
well-informed patients who are aware of the benefits, costs, and risks of all available payment options,
including financial assistance, prior making important decisions about medical care and treatments.
PayZen is committed to advancing equitable access to healthcare and is demonstrating that
patient-friendly payment options are both available and economically feasible. By contrast, payment
products that charge interest and fees leave consumers worse off and do not benefit the community or
assist patients financially.
III.
Conclusion
PayZen appreciates the Agencies’ focus on these important issues and encourages the Agencies to use
their respective authorities to address the healthcare affordability crisis. Should the Agencies have further
questions they may contact me at itzik@payzen.com.
Sincerely,
Itzik Cohen
Co-Founder and CEO, PayZen

Title: Comment from National Disability Institute
Comment: See attached file(s)
Attachment Files_extracted:
Re: Request for Information Regarding Specialty Medical Payment Products Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 1700 G Street NW Washington, DC 20552
Assistive technology helps to meet the medical need of a person who has a disability or a medical condition.  A modified vehicle is a form of assistive technology that helps to address the medical needs for those who rely on private transportation to get themselves to their jobs, medical appointments and to commute within their communities.  
The 2017 National Household Travel Survey, indicates that 25.5 million people over age five report disabilities that limit their ability to travel [2]. Of these, 11.6% use a manual wheelchair, 3.9% use power wheelchairs, and 4.4% use scooters, indicating that about 5 million people use wheeled mobility devices in the USA. Many of these people own modified vehicles. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8835052/
There are limited modified vehicle dealerships nationally that address this volume of assistive technology by providing modified vehicles. There is not significant competition within this industry, and it is thought that price gouging is a common practice among these dealerships. There are reports of modified caravans selling for $120,000. When competition is limited, there is little opportunity for cost comparison. On top of that, many dealers advertise interest at 4%, when in fact, many of the vehicle purchase loans are issued at 18% or higher with repayment terms that may be up to 10 or 15 years. Such selling points and terms make these vehicles unaffordable.
Modified vehicle sites list wheelchair accessible vehicles, that have more than 150,000 miles with modifications having been done more than 10 years ago, starting at a selling price of $40,000 or more. What is the true value of those vehicles that had those modifications completed ten or more years ago? Conventional financing through banks and credit unions is not available for modified vehicles when the blue-book value does not equate to the vehicle’s base value. This AT vendor financing leads to lending terms with interest rates from 18% to 30% with extended repayment terms of up to 10 or 15 years.
Facilities that provide hand-control conversion of vehicles are even more limited. There are hand control assessments and costs that range from $1,200 - $30,000 to $60,000 or more. The limited competition again results in hand control conversion interest rates at 18% or more. Even for people who have credit scores in the range of 720 to 825, interest rates are commonly offered at 18% interest or higher. 
There are also instances when modified vehicles are sold, without reasonable repairs being done prior to sale. Some consumers purchase extended warranties or rely on pre-purchase evaluations in an effort to protect themselves when purchasing a modified vehicle, adding more cost to the purchase. 


Recommendation: There should be a standard appraised value for common vehicle modification features. Modifications that were completed years ago should be subject to depreciation. Kelly Blue Book should address these values within their auto guide; this could help customers know the value of modified vehicles. 

There are many other forms of assistive technology (AT) such as hearing or vision aids that may or may not be included within health care policies. Many times, AT vendors rely on CareCredit or other finance companies that offer 0% interest for up to 6 months. For customers who qualify for this line of credit, who manage to pay on time each of those 6 months, that works out fine. But, if a payment is missed or the loan is not paid in full within 6 months, an interest rate of 18% is applied upon the original loan amount, dated back to the date of loan / credit origination. This practice makes AT, a form of a medical product, unaffordable.

Thank you for the opportunity to submit comments!

Respectfully Submitted, Laurie Schaller
Manager Financial Empowerment
National Disability Institute, Assistive Technology Loan Program / New Jersey and New York

Title: Comment from Georgia Watch
Comment: In response to the Request for Information Regarding Medical Products (RFI) issued by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), and the Department of the Treasury (Treasury), we submit the attached comments on behalf of the more than 100,000 Georgians burdened with medical debt. We are pleased that your Agencies are seeking to understand the effects that medical credit cards, loans, and other financial products have on patients and the healthcare system. Georgians need real solutions already available to your Agencies to prevent the incurrence of debilitating debt when accessing needed healthcare. Thank you for your consideration of the attached comments.
Attachment Files_extracted:
55 Marietta Street NW    Suite 903    Atlanta GA  30303    [P] 404.525.1085   georgiawatch.org 
 
September 11, 2023 
 Via Electronic Submission Only: www.regulations.gov 
  
Director Rohit Chopra, Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 
Secretary Xavier Becerra, Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services 
Department of Health and Human Services 
Secretary Janet Yellen, Department of the Treasury 
  
Re: Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products, 
CFPB-2023-0038 / CMS-2023-0106 / TREAS-DO-2023-0008 
  
Dear Director Chopra, Secretary Becerra, and Secretary Yellen: 
  
We submit the following comments in response to the Request for Information Regarding 
Medical Products (RFI) issued by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), the 
Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), and the Department of the Treasury 
(Treasury). We are pleased that your Agencies are seeking to understand the effects that 
medical credit cards, loans, and other financial products have on patients and the 
healthcare system. 
 
Georgia Watch is a consumer advocacy organization with a long history of advocating in 
the financial protection and healthcare access space. Our team works to ensure barriers 
to quality, affordable healthcare are removed for all Georgians. Through our consumer 
helpline, we often hear stories of individuals struggling to maneuver the intricacies of the 
healthcare system, particularly when it comes to billing and debt. According to the Kaiser 
Family Foundation, over 100,000 Georgians have more than $120 million in medical debt. 
From our direct experience assisting consumers, we know that many of these debt-
burdened individuals would have qualified for financial assistance but were not offered it. 
Georgians need real solutions already available to your Agencies to prevent the 
incurrence of debilitating debt when accessing needed healthcare.  
 
We share the concerns of our partners at the National Consumer Law Center (NCLC) and 
Community Catalyst that medical payment products are further complicating an already 
complex process to receive and pay for needed care. We are particularly concerned 
about NCLC’s research indicating a higher susceptibility of vulnerable populations to use 
these more expensive products, including older adults and immigrant communities. 
Seeking basic and emergency care should not be an entry point for consumers to get 
swept into misleading and unnecessary products and services.  

 
 
 
 
55 Marietta Street NW    Suite 903    Atlanta GA  30303    [P] 404.525.1085   georgiawatch.org 
 
 
Medical credit cards often come with high interest rates and hidden fees, which can swiftly 
escalate the overall cost of medical procedures and services. Moreover, the complex 
terms and conditions of these cards can be misleading, leaving consumers vulnerable to 
accumulating debt they may struggle to repay and potentially impacting their credit 
scores. While medical credit cards can offer financial relief in times of medical need, they 
also demand careful consideration and informed decision-making to avoid the potentially 
detrimental consequences of excessive debt and financial strain. 
 
Consumers in Georgia have filed numerous complaints with the CFPB expressing that 
interest rates attached to these products are so high that it makes it difficult to move the 
needle on the actual debt owed. Georgians also share that when applying for medical 
credit cards, such as CareCredit, hidden promotions result in exponential increases in 
interest after the promotional period. 
 
We agree with solutions proposed by Community Catalyst and partners and ask that the 
CFPB: (1) prohibit the offering and promotion of deferred interest credit cards in health 
care settings; and (2) ban the inclusion of all medical debt on credit reports. We ask that 
HHS: (1) enact Conditions of Participation to reduce hospital debt and prohibit the most 
harmful collection actions by hospitals; and (2) address high rates of denials by private 
insurance companies and by Medicaid managed care organizations (Medicaid MCOs). 
Finally, we ask that Treasury: (1) strengthen and enforce current regulations for nonprofit 
hospitals relating to financial assistance policies and collection actions; and (2) reinstate 
previous guidance requiring nonprofit hospitals to provide charity care. 
 
Georgia Watch and our partners are committed to ensuring that consumers can access 
necessary medical care without the fear of insurmountable debt. We thank the Agencies 
for their coordinated efforts to gain information regarding medical payment products. We 
urge the Agencies to continue prioritizing collaboration in addressing the underlying causes 
of medical debt.  
 
On behalf of Georgia consumers, thank you for your consideration of these comments. 
 
Sincerely,  
Elizabeth B. Coyle 
Executive Director

Title: Comment submitted by Anders Gilberg
Comment: See Attached
Attachment Files_extracted:
1717 Pennsylvania Ave. NW, #600  .  Washington, DC 20006  .  T 202.293.3450  .  F 202.293.2787  .  mgma.com  
 
 
 
September 11, 2023 
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau  
1700 G Street, NW 
Washington, DC 20552 
 
Re: Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products, Docket No. CFPB-2023-0038 
To Whom it May Concern: 
On behalf of our member medical group practices, the Medical Group Management Association 
(MGMA) thanks you for the opportunity to comment on the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, 
Department of Health and Human Services, and Department of Treasury’s (collectively the agencies) 
joint Request for Information (RFI) regarding medical payment products. MGMA appreciates the 
agencies’ interest in seeking to understand the effects of medical payment products on patients and the 
healthcare system. As healthcare leaders, we want to underscore our commitment to providing high-
quality, cost-effective, and timely patient care. Any utilization of these products is done with this in mind. 
MGMA is pleased to share feedback on how these payment products intersect with group practices.    
With a membership of more than 60,000 medical practice administrators, executives, and leaders, MGMA 
represents more than 15,000 medical groups in which more than 350,000 physicians practice. These 
groups range from small private practices in rural areas to large regional and national health systems, and 
cover the full spectrum of physician specialties and organizational forms, making MGMA well-positioned 
to offer the following feedback. 
 
The challenges of running a successful medical practice continue to grow, given the current environment 
of staffing shortages, inflation, and Medicare payment cuts. According to the American Medical 
Association (AMA), the cost of running a medical practice increased 47% from 2001 to 2023 while 
Medicare physician payments increased just 9%.1 Medicare physician payment effectively decreased by 
26% from 2001 to 2023 when adjusted for inflation in practice costs. Costs continue to grow as 89% of 
medical groups reported an increase in operating costs in a July 13, 2023, MGMA poll.2 Ensuring access 
to medical care is paramount and timely receipt of payment is a critical aspect of keeping physician 
practice doors open. Medical payment products serve this function for practices that choose to utilize 
these services.  
The RFI asks questions about potential kickbacks to providers from medical payment companies for 
offering certain financial products to patients. It is our understanding that the opposite is true – providers 
pay these companies to offer services to receive timely payment, reduce administrative costs, and most 
importantly, expand access for patients without the immediate financial means to pay. The alternative  –  
 
1 AMA, Medicare updates compared to inflation (2001 – 2023), https://www.ama-assn.org/system/files/ama-medicare-gaps-chart-grassroots-
insert.pdf. 
2 MGMA stat poll, July 13, 2023, https://www.mgma.com/mgma-stat/higher-costs-persist-for-medical-groups-even-as-inflations-growth-slows.  

 
 
1717 Pennsylvania Ave. NW, #600  .  Washington, DC 20006  .  T 202.293.3450  .  F 202.293.2787  .  mgma.com  
 
hiring additional administrative staff to facilitate payment plans and collections  –  is not economically 
feasible for many groups, especially small and rural ones.  
The agencies discuss the history of medical payment products and characterize their use as having been 
primarily for elective procedures. Our understanding is that most of these products are still used for this 
purpose as well as for dental services. Since elective procedures are not always covered by insurance, 
financing options are an important tool to pay for care received. We are concerned, however, that this RFI 
appears to perpetuate the notion it is acceptable for payment not to be rendered immediately after clinical 
services are provided, and it’s ok that payments are often subject to significant delays. This belief simply 
shifts the burden of the shortcomings of the U.S. healthcare system onto providers. It’s no surprise under 
this notion that the majority of physicians in the U.S. are now employees of larger health systems, having 
found it impossible to survive as a small business in private practice.  
In the end, some healthcare credit products offer more patient-friendly options compared to commercial 
credit cards, such as deferred interest financing that spreads out repayment over a defined timeframe with 
no interest. For qualifying patients, these financing options can help ensure they are able to make 
affordable payments over a timeframe that works for them. Misrepresentations about terms and 
conditions, interest rates, and other financing options have no place in healthcare, just as they have no 
place in commercial credit products. We often hear of patients using these products knowingly and 
without undue pressure – some of our own members have told us they use these products as patients 
themselves.  
We appreciate the agencies’ dedication to protecting the integrity of the healthcare system as predatory 
actors give a bad name to the rest of providers committed to treating patients. If you have any questions, 
please contact James Haynes, Associate Director of Government Affairs, at jhaynes@mgma.org or 202-
293-3450.  
Sincerely, 
/s/ 
Anders Gilberg 
Senior Vice President, Government Affairs
